speeches.speech: Boatswain!
lines: Boatswain!

speeches.speech: Here, master: what cheer?
lines: Here, master: what cheer?

speeches.speech: Good, speak to the mariners: fall to't, yarely, or we run ourselves aground: bestir, bestir.
lines: Good, speak to the mariners: fall to't, yarely,
or we run ourselves aground: bestir, bestir.

speeches.speech: Heigh, my hearts! cheerly, cheerly, my hearts! yare, yare! Take in the topsail. Tend to the master's whistle. Blow, till thou burst thy wind, if room enough! [Enter ALONSO, SEBASTIAN, ANTONIO, FERDINAND,] GONZALO, and others]
lines: Heigh, my hearts! cheerly, cheerly, my hearts!
yare, yare! Take in the topsail. Tend to the
master's whistle. Blow, till thou burst thy wind,
if room enough!
[Enter ALONSO, SEBASTIAN, ANTONIO, FERDINAND,]
GONZALO, and others]

speeches.speech: Good boatswain, have care. Where's the master? Play the men.
lines: Good boatswain, have care. Where's the master?
Play the men.

speeches.speech: I pray now, keep below.
lines: I pray now, keep below.

speeches.speech: Where is the master, boatswain?
lines: Where is the master, boatswain?

speeches.speech: Do you not hear him? You mar our labour: keep your cabins: you do assist the storm.
lines: Do you not hear him? You mar our labour: keep your
cabins: you do assist the storm.

speeches.speech: Nay, good, be patient.
lines: Nay, good, be patient.

speeches.speech: When the sea is. Hence! What cares these roarers for the name of king? To cabin: silence! trouble us not.
lines: When the sea is. Hence! What cares these roarers
for the name of king? To cabin: silence! trouble us not.

speeches.speech: Good, yet remember whom thou hast aboard.
lines: Good, yet remember whom thou hast aboard.

speeches.speech: None that I more love than myself. You are a counsellor; if you can command these elements to silence, and work the peace of the present, we will not hand a rope more; use your authority: if you cannot, give thanks you have lived so long, and make yourself ready in your cabin for the mischance of the hour, if it so hap. Cheerly, good hearts! Out of our way, I say.
lines: None that I more love than myself. You are a
counsellor; if you can command these elements to
silence, and work the peace of the present, we will
not hand a rope more; use your authority: if you
cannot, give thanks you have lived so long, and make
yourself ready in your cabin for the mischance of
the hour, if it so hap. Cheerly, good hearts! Out
of our way, I say.

speeches.speech: I have great comfort from this fellow: methinks he hath no drowning mark upon him; his complexion is perfect gallows. Stand fast, good Fate, to his hanging: make the rope of his destiny our cable, for our own doth little advantage. If he be not born to be hanged, our case is miserable.
lines: I have great comfort from this fellow: methinks he
hath no drowning mark upon him; his complexion is
perfect gallows. Stand fast, good Fate, to his
hanging: make the rope of his destiny our cable,
for our own doth little advantage. If he be not
born to be hanged, our case is miserable.

speeches.speech: Down with the topmast! yare! lower, lower! Bring her to try with main-course. [A cry within] A plague upon this howling! they are louder than the weather or our office. [Re-enter SEBASTIAN, ANTONIO, and GONZALO] Yet again! what do you here? Shall we give o'er and drown? Have you a mind to sink?
lines: Down with the topmast! yare! lower, lower! Bring
her to try with main-course.
[A cry within]
A plague upon this howling! they are louder than
the weather or our office.
[Re-enter SEBASTIAN, ANTONIO, and GONZALO]
Yet again! what do you here? Shall we give o'er
and drown? Have you a mind to sink?

speeches.speech: A pox o' your throat, you bawling, blasphemous, incharitable dog!
lines: A pox o' your throat, you bawling, blasphemous,
incharitable dog!

speeches.speech: Work you then.
lines: Work you then.

speeches.speech: Hang, cur! hang, you whoreson, insolent noisemaker! We are less afraid to be drowned than thou art.
lines: Hang, cur! hang, you whoreson, insolent noisemaker!
We are less afraid to be drowned than thou art.

speeches.speech: I'll warrant him for drowning; though the ship were no stronger than a nutshell and as leaky as an unstanched wench.
lines: I'll warrant him for drowning; though the ship were
no stronger than a nutshell and as leaky as an
unstanched wench.

speeches.speech: Lay her a-hold, a-hold! set her two courses off to sea again; lay her off.
lines: Lay her a-hold, a-hold! set her two courses off to
sea again; lay her off.

speeches.speech: All lost! to prayers, to prayers! all lost!
lines: All lost! to prayers, to prayers! all lost!

speeches.speech: What, must our mouths be cold?
lines: What, must our mouths be cold?

speeches.speech: The king and prince at prayers! let's assist them, For our case is as theirs.
lines: The king and prince at prayers! let's assist them,
For our case is as theirs.

speeches.speech: I'm out of patience.
lines: I'm out of patience.

speeches.speech: We are merely cheated of our lives by drunkards: This wide-chapp'd rascal--would thou mightst lie drowning The washing of ten tides!
lines: We are merely cheated of our lives by drunkards:
This wide-chapp'd rascal--would thou mightst lie drowning
The washing of ten tides!

speeches.speech: He'll be hang'd yet, Though every drop of water swear against it And gape at widest to glut him. [A confused noise within: 'Mercy on us!'--] 'We split, we split!'--'Farewell, my wife and children!'-- 'Farewell, brother!'--'We split, we split, we split!']
lines: He'll be hang'd yet,
Though every drop of water swear against it
And gape at widest to glut him.
[A confused noise within: 'Mercy on us!'--]
'We split, we split!'--'Farewell, my wife and
children!'--
'Farewell, brother!'--'We split, we split, we split!']

speeches.speech: Let's all sink with the king.
lines: Let's all sink with the king.

speeches.speech: Let's take leave of him.
lines: Let's take leave of him.

speeches.speech: Now would I give a thousand furlongs of sea for an acre of barren ground, long heath, brown furze, any thing. The wills above be done! but I would fain die a dry death.
lines: Now would I give a thousand furlongs of sea for an
acre of barren ground, long heath, brown furze, any
thing. The wills above be done! but I would fain
die a dry death.

speeches.speech: If by your art, my dearest father, you have Put the wild waters in this roar, allay them. The sky, it seems, would pour down stinking pitch, But that the sea, mounting to the welkin's cheek, Dashes the fire out. O, I have suffered With those that I saw suffer: a brave vessel, Who had, no doubt, some noble creature in her, Dash'd all to pieces. O, the cry did knock Against my very heart. Poor souls, they perish'd. Had I been any god of power, I would Have sunk the sea within the earth or ere It should the good ship so have swallow'd and The fraughting souls within her.
lines: If by your art, my dearest father, you have
Put the wild waters in this roar, allay them.
The sky, it seems, would pour down stinking pitch,
But that the sea, mounting to the welkin's cheek,
Dashes the fire out. O, I have suffered
With those that I saw suffer: a brave vessel,
Who had, no doubt, some noble creature in her,
Dash'd all to pieces. O, the cry did knock
Against my very heart. Poor souls, they perish'd.
Had I been any god of power, I would
Have sunk the sea within the earth or ere
It should the good ship so have swallow'd and
The fraughting souls within her.

speeches.speech: Be collected: No more amazement: tell your piteous heart There's no harm done.
lines: Be collected:
No more amazement: tell your piteous heart
There's no harm done.

speeches.speech: O, woe the day!
lines: O, woe the day!

speeches.speech: No harm. I have done nothing but in care of thee, Of thee, my dear one, thee, my daughter, who Art ignorant of what thou art, nought knowing Of whence I am, nor that I am more better Than Prospero, master of a full poor cell, And thy no greater father.
lines: No harm.
I have done nothing but in care of thee,
Of thee, my dear one, thee, my daughter, who
Art ignorant of what thou art, nought knowing
Of whence I am, nor that I am more better
Than Prospero, master of a full poor cell,
And thy no greater father.

speeches.speech: More to know Did never meddle with my thoughts.
lines: More to know
Did never meddle with my thoughts.

speeches.speech: 'Tis time I should inform thee farther. Lend thy hand, And pluck my magic garment from me. So: [Lays down his mantle] Lie there, my art. Wipe thou thine eyes; have comfort. The direful spectacle of the wreck, which touch'd The very virtue of compassion in thee, I have with such provision in mine art So safely ordered that there is no soul-- No, not so much perdition as an hair Betid to any creature in the vessel Which thou heard'st cry, which thou saw'st sink. Sit down; For thou must now know farther.
lines: 'Tis time
I should inform thee farther. Lend thy hand,
And pluck my magic garment from me. So:
[Lays down his mantle]
Lie there, my art. Wipe thou thine eyes; have comfort.
The direful spectacle of the wreck, which touch'd
The very virtue of compassion in thee,
I have with such provision in mine art
So safely ordered that there is no soul--
No, not so much perdition as an hair
Betid to any creature in the vessel
Which thou heard'st cry, which thou saw'st sink. Sit down;
For thou must now know farther.

speeches.speech: You have often Begun to tell me what I am, but stopp'd And left me to a bootless inquisition, Concluding 'Stay: not yet.'
lines: You have often
Begun to tell me what I am, but stopp'd
And left me to a bootless inquisition,
Concluding 'Stay: not yet.'

speeches.speech: The hour's now come; The very minute bids thee ope thine ear; Obey and be attentive. Canst thou remember A time before we came unto this cell? I do not think thou canst, for then thou wast not Out three years old.
lines: The hour's now come;
The very minute bids thee ope thine ear;
Obey and be attentive. Canst thou remember
A time before we came unto this cell?
I do not think thou canst, for then thou wast not
Out three years old.

speeches.speech: Certainly, sir, I can.
lines: Certainly, sir, I can.

speeches.speech: By what? by any other house or person? Of any thing the image tell me that Hath kept with thy remembrance.
lines: By what? by any other house or person?
Of any thing the image tell me that
Hath kept with thy remembrance.

speeches.speech: 'Tis far off And rather like a dream than an assurance That my remembrance warrants. Had I not Four or five women once that tended me?
lines: 'Tis far off
And rather like a dream than an assurance
That my remembrance warrants. Had I not
Four or five women once that tended me?

speeches.speech: Thou hadst, and more, Miranda. But how is it That this lives in thy mind? What seest thou else In the dark backward and abysm of time? If thou remember'st aught ere thou camest here, How thou camest here thou mayst.
lines: Thou hadst, and more, Miranda. But how is it
That this lives in thy mind? What seest thou else
In the dark backward and abysm of time?
If thou remember'st aught ere thou camest here,
How thou camest here thou mayst.

speeches.speech: But that I do not.
lines: But that I do not.

speeches.speech: Twelve year since, Miranda, twelve year since, Thy father was the Duke of Milan and A prince of power.
lines: Twelve year since, Miranda, twelve year since,
Thy father was the Duke of Milan and
A prince of power.

speeches.speech: Sir, are not you my father?
lines: Sir, are not you my father?

speeches.speech: Thy mother was a piece of virtue, and She said thou wast my daughter; and thy father Was Duke of Milan; and thou his only heir And princess no worse issued.
lines: Thy mother was a piece of virtue, and
She said thou wast my daughter; and thy father
Was Duke of Milan; and thou his only heir
And princess no worse issued.

speeches.speech: O the heavens! What foul play had we, that we came from thence? Or blessed was't we did?
lines: O the heavens!
What foul play had we, that we came from thence?
Or blessed was't we did?

speeches.speech: Both, both, my girl: By foul play, as thou say'st, were we heaved thence, But blessedly holp hither.
lines: Both, both, my girl:
By foul play, as thou say'st, were we heaved thence,
But blessedly holp hither.

speeches.speech: O, my heart bleeds To think o' the teen that I have turn'd you to, Which is from my remembrance! Please you, farther.
lines: O, my heart bleeds
To think o' the teen that I have turn'd you to,
Which is from my remembrance! Please you, farther.

speeches.speech: My brother and thy uncle, call'd Antonio-- I pray thee, mark me--that a brother should Be so perfidious!--he whom next thyself Of all the world I loved and to him put The manage of my state; as at that time Through all the signories it was the first And Prospero the prime duke, being so reputed In dignity, and for the liberal arts Without a parallel; those being all my study, The government I cast upon my brother And to my state grew stranger, being transported And rapt in secret studies. Thy false uncle-- Dost thou attend me?
lines: My brother and thy uncle, call'd Antonio--
I pray thee, mark me--that a brother should
Be so perfidious!--he whom next thyself
Of all the world I loved and to him put
The manage of my state; as at that time
Through all the signories it was the first
And Prospero the prime duke, being so reputed
In dignity, and for the liberal arts
Without a parallel; those being all my study,
The government I cast upon my brother
And to my state grew stranger, being transported
And rapt in secret studies. Thy false uncle--
Dost thou attend me?

speeches.speech: Sir, most heedfully.
lines: Sir, most heedfully.

speeches.speech: Being once perfected how to grant suits, How to deny them, who to advance and who To trash for over-topping, new created The creatures that were mine, I say, or changed 'em, Or else new form'd 'em; having both the key Of officer and office, set all hearts i' the state To what tune pleased his ear; that now he was The ivy which had hid my princely trunk, And suck'd my verdure out on't. Thou attend'st not.
lines: Being once perfected how to grant suits,
How to deny them, who to advance and who
To trash for over-topping, new created
The creatures that were mine, I say, or changed 'em,
Or else new form'd 'em; having both the key
Of officer and office, set all hearts i' the state
To what tune pleased his ear; that now he was
The ivy which had hid my princely trunk,
And suck'd my verdure out on't. Thou attend'st not.

speeches.speech: O, good sir, I do.
lines: O, good sir, I do.

speeches.speech: I pray thee, mark me. I, thus neglecting worldly ends, all dedicated To closeness and the bettering of my mind With that which, but by being so retired, O'er-prized all popular rate, in my false brother Awaked an evil nature; and my trust, Like a good parent, did beget of him A falsehood in its contrary as great As my trust was; which had indeed no limit, A confidence sans bound. He being thus lorded, Not only with what my revenue yielded, But what my power might else exact, like one Who having into truth, by telling of it, Made such a sinner of his memory, To credit his own lie, he did believe He was indeed the duke; out o' the substitution And executing the outward face of royalty, With all prerogative: hence his ambition growing-- Dost thou hear?
lines: I pray thee, mark me.
I, thus neglecting worldly ends, all dedicated
To closeness and the bettering of my mind
With that which, but by being so retired,
O'er-prized all popular rate, in my false brother
Awaked an evil nature; and my trust,
Like a good parent, did beget of him
A falsehood in its contrary as great
As my trust was; which had indeed no limit,
A confidence sans bound. He being thus lorded,
Not only with what my revenue yielded,
But what my power might else exact, like one
Who having into truth, by telling of it,
Made such a sinner of his memory,
To credit his own lie, he did believe
He was indeed the duke; out o' the substitution
And executing the outward face of royalty,
With all prerogative: hence his ambition growing--
Dost thou hear?

speeches.speech: Your tale, sir, would cure deafness.
lines: Your tale, sir, would cure deafness.

speeches.speech: To have no screen between this part he play'd And him he play'd it for, he needs will be Absolute Milan. Me, poor man, my library Was dukedom large enough: of temporal royalties He thinks me now incapable; confederates-- So dry he was for sway--wi' the King of Naples To give him annual tribute, do him homage, Subject his coronet to his crown and bend The dukedom yet unbow'd--alas, poor Milan!-- To most ignoble stooping.
lines: To have no screen between this part he play'd
And him he play'd it for, he needs will be
Absolute Milan. Me, poor man, my library
Was dukedom large enough: of temporal royalties
He thinks me now incapable; confederates--
So dry he was for sway--wi' the King of Naples
To give him annual tribute, do him homage,
Subject his coronet to his crown and bend
The dukedom yet unbow'd--alas, poor Milan!--
To most ignoble stooping.

speeches.speech: O the heavens!
lines: O the heavens!

speeches.speech: Mark his condition and the event; then tell me If this might be a brother.
lines: Mark his condition and the event; then tell me
If this might be a brother.

speeches.speech: I should sin To think but nobly of my grandmother: Good wombs have borne bad sons.
lines: I should sin
To think but nobly of my grandmother:
Good wombs have borne bad sons.

speeches.speech: Now the condition. The King of Naples, being an enemy To me inveterate, hearkens my brother's suit; Which was, that he, in lieu o' the premises Of homage and I know not how much tribute, Should presently extirpate me and mine Out of the dukedom and confer fair Milan With all the honours on my brother: whereon, A treacherous army levied, one midnight Fated to the purpose did Antonio open The gates of Milan, and, i' the dead of darkness, The ministers for the purpose hurried thence Me and thy crying self.
lines: Now the condition.
The King of Naples, being an enemy
To me inveterate, hearkens my brother's suit;
Which was, that he, in lieu o' the premises
Of homage and I know not how much tribute,
Should presently extirpate me and mine
Out of the dukedom and confer fair Milan
With all the honours on my brother: whereon,
A treacherous army levied, one midnight
Fated to the purpose did Antonio open
The gates of Milan, and, i' the dead of darkness,
The ministers for the purpose hurried thence
Me and thy crying self.

speeches.speech: Alack, for pity! I, not remembering how I cried out then, Will cry it o'er again: it is a hint That wrings mine eyes to't.
lines: Alack, for pity!
I, not remembering how I cried out then,
Will cry it o'er again: it is a hint
That wrings mine eyes to't.

speeches.speech: Hear a little further And then I'll bring thee to the present business Which now's upon's; without the which this story Were most impertinent.
lines: Hear a little further
And then I'll bring thee to the present business
Which now's upon's; without the which this story
Were most impertinent.

speeches.speech: Wherefore did they not That hour destroy us?
lines: Wherefore did they not
That hour destroy us?

speeches.speech: Well demanded, wench: My tale provokes that question. Dear, they durst not, So dear the love my people bore me, nor set A mark so bloody on the business, but With colours fairer painted their foul ends. In few, they hurried us aboard a bark, Bore us some leagues to sea; where they prepared A rotten carcass of a boat, not rigg'd, Nor tackle, sail, nor mast; the very rats Instinctively had quit it: there they hoist us, To cry to the sea that roar'd to us, to sigh To the winds whose pity, sighing back again, Did us but loving wrong.
lines: Well demanded, wench:
My tale provokes that question. Dear, they durst not,
So dear the love my people bore me, nor set
A mark so bloody on the business, but
With colours fairer painted their foul ends.
In few, they hurried us aboard a bark,
Bore us some leagues to sea; where they prepared
A rotten carcass of a boat, not rigg'd,
Nor tackle, sail, nor mast; the very rats
Instinctively had quit it: there they hoist us,
To cry to the sea that roar'd to us, to sigh
To the winds whose pity, sighing back again,
Did us but loving wrong.

speeches.speech: Alack, what trouble Was I then to you!
lines: Alack, what trouble
Was I then to you!

speeches.speech: O, a cherubim Thou wast that did preserve me. Thou didst smile. Infused with a fortitude from heaven, When I have deck'd the sea with drops full salt, Under my burthen groan'd; which raised in me An undergoing stomach, to bear up Against what should ensue.
lines: O, a cherubim
Thou wast that did preserve me. Thou didst smile.
Infused with a fortitude from heaven,
When I have deck'd the sea with drops full salt,
Under my burthen groan'd; which raised in me
An undergoing stomach, to bear up
Against what should ensue.

speeches.speech: How came we ashore?
lines: How came we ashore?

speeches.speech: By Providence divine. Some food we had and some fresh water that A noble Neapolitan, Gonzalo, Out of his charity, being then appointed Master of this design, did give us, with Rich garments, linens, stuffs and necessaries, Which since have steaded much; so, of his gentleness, Knowing I loved my books, he furnish'd me From mine own library with volumes that I prize above my dukedom.
lines: By Providence divine.
Some food we had and some fresh water that
A noble Neapolitan, Gonzalo,
Out of his charity, being then appointed
Master of this design, did give us, with
Rich garments, linens, stuffs and necessaries,
Which since have steaded much; so, of his gentleness,
Knowing I loved my books, he furnish'd me
From mine own library with volumes that
I prize above my dukedom.

speeches.speech: Would I might But ever see that man!
lines: Would I might
But ever see that man!

speeches.speech: Now I arise: [Resumes his mantle] Sit still, and hear the last of our sea-sorrow. Here in this island we arrived; and here Have I, thy schoolmaster, made thee more profit Than other princesses can that have more time For vainer hours and tutors not so careful.
lines: Now I arise:
[Resumes his mantle]
Sit still, and hear the last of our sea-sorrow.
Here in this island we arrived; and here
Have I, thy schoolmaster, made thee more profit
Than other princesses can that have more time
For vainer hours and tutors not so careful.

speeches.speech: Heavens thank you for't! And now, I pray you, sir, For still 'tis beating in my mind, your reason For raising this sea-storm?
lines: Heavens thank you for't! And now, I pray you, sir,
For still 'tis beating in my mind, your reason
For raising this sea-storm?

speeches.speech: Know thus far forth. By accident most strange, bountiful Fortune, Now my dear lady, hath mine enemies Brought to this shore; and by my prescience I find my zenith doth depend upon A most auspicious star, whose influence If now I court not but omit, my fortunes Will ever after droop. Here cease more questions: Thou art inclined to sleep; 'tis a good dulness, And give it way: I know thou canst not choose. [MIRANDA sleeps] Come away, servant, come. I am ready now. Approach, my Ariel, come.
lines: Know thus far forth.
By accident most strange, bountiful Fortune,
Now my dear lady, hath mine enemies
Brought to this shore; and by my prescience
I find my zenith doth depend upon
A most auspicious star, whose influence
If now I court not but omit, my fortunes
Will ever after droop. Here cease more questions:
Thou art inclined to sleep; 'tis a good dulness,
And give it way: I know thou canst not choose.
[MIRANDA sleeps]
Come away, servant, come. I am ready now.
Approach, my Ariel, come.

speeches.speech: All hail, great master! grave sir, hail! I come To answer thy best pleasure; be't to fly, To swim, to dive into the fire, to ride On the curl'd clouds, to thy strong bidding task Ariel and all his quality.
lines: All hail, great master! grave sir, hail! I come
To answer thy best pleasure; be't to fly,
To swim, to dive into the fire, to ride
On the curl'd clouds, to thy strong bidding task
Ariel and all his quality.

speeches.speech: Hast thou, spirit, Perform'd to point the tempest that I bade thee?
lines: Hast thou, spirit,
Perform'd to point the tempest that I bade thee?

speeches.speech: To every article. I boarded the king's ship; now on the beak, Now in the waist, the deck, in every cabin, I flamed amazement: sometime I'ld divide, And burn in many places; on the topmast, The yards and bowsprit, would I flame distinctly, Then meet and join. Jove's lightnings, the precursors O' the dreadful thunder-claps, more momentary And sight-outrunning were not; the fire and cracks Of sulphurous roaring the most mighty Neptune Seem to besiege and make his bold waves tremble, Yea, his dread trident shake.
lines: To every article.
I boarded the king's ship; now on the beak,
Now in the waist, the deck, in every cabin,
I flamed amazement: sometime I'ld divide,
And burn in many places; on the topmast,
The yards and bowsprit, would I flame distinctly,
Then meet and join. Jove's lightnings, the precursors
O' the dreadful thunder-claps, more momentary
And sight-outrunning were not; the fire and cracks
Of sulphurous roaring the most mighty Neptune
Seem to besiege and make his bold waves tremble,
Yea, his dread trident shake.

speeches.speech: My brave spirit! Who was so firm, so constant, that this coil Would not infect his reason?
lines: My brave spirit!
Who was so firm, so constant, that this coil
Would not infect his reason?

speeches.speech: Not a soul But felt a fever of the mad and play'd Some tricks of desperation. All but mariners Plunged in the foaming brine and quit the vessel, Then all afire with me: the king's son, Ferdinand, With hair up-staring,--then like reeds, not hair,-- Was the first man that leap'd; cried, 'Hell is empty And all the devils are here.'
lines: Not a soul
But felt a fever of the mad and play'd
Some tricks of desperation. All but mariners
Plunged in the foaming brine and quit the vessel,
Then all afire with me: the king's son, Ferdinand,
With hair up-staring,--then like reeds, not hair,--
Was the first man that leap'd; cried, 'Hell is empty
And all the devils are here.'

speeches.speech: Why that's my spirit! But was not this nigh shore?
lines: Why that's my spirit!
But was not this nigh shore?

speeches.speech: Close by, my master.
lines: Close by, my master.

speeches.speech: But are they, Ariel, safe?
lines: But are they, Ariel, safe?

speeches.speech: Not a hair perish'd; On their sustaining garments not a blemish, But fresher than before: and, as thou badest me, In troops I have dispersed them 'bout the isle. The king's son have I landed by himself; Whom I left cooling of the air with sighs In an odd angle of the isle and sitting, His arms in this sad knot.
lines: Not a hair perish'd;
On their sustaining garments not a blemish,
But fresher than before: and, as thou badest me,
In troops I have dispersed them 'bout the isle.
The king's son have I landed by himself;
Whom I left cooling of the air with sighs
In an odd angle of the isle and sitting,
His arms in this sad knot.

speeches.speech: Of the king's ship The mariners say how thou hast disposed And all the rest o' the fleet.
lines: Of the king's ship
The mariners say how thou hast disposed
And all the rest o' the fleet.

speeches.speech: Safely in harbour Is the king's ship; in the deep nook, where once Thou call'dst me up at midnight to fetch dew From the still-vex'd Bermoothes, there she's hid: The mariners all under hatches stow'd; Who, with a charm join'd to their suffer'd labour, I have left asleep; and for the rest o' the fleet Which I dispersed, they all have met again And are upon the Mediterranean flote, Bound sadly home for Naples, Supposing that they saw the king's ship wreck'd And his great person perish.
lines: Safely in harbour
Is the king's ship; in the deep nook, where once
Thou call'dst me up at midnight to fetch dew
From the still-vex'd Bermoothes, there she's hid:
The mariners all under hatches stow'd;
Who, with a charm join'd to their suffer'd labour,
I have left asleep; and for the rest o' the fleet
Which I dispersed, they all have met again
And are upon the Mediterranean flote,
Bound sadly home for Naples,
Supposing that they saw the king's ship wreck'd
And his great person perish.

speeches.speech: Ariel, thy charge Exactly is perform'd: but there's more work. What is the time o' the day?
lines: Ariel, thy charge
Exactly is perform'd: but there's more work.
What is the time o' the day?

speeches.speech: Past the mid season.
lines: Past the mid season.

speeches.speech: At least two glasses. The time 'twixt six and now Must by us both be spent most preciously.
lines: At least two glasses. The time 'twixt six and now
Must by us both be spent most preciously.

speeches.speech: Is there more toil? Since thou dost give me pains, Let me remember thee what thou hast promised, Which is not yet perform'd me.
lines: Is there more toil? Since thou dost give me pains,
Let me remember thee what thou hast promised,
Which is not yet perform'd me.

speeches.speech: How now? moody? What is't thou canst demand?
lines: How now? moody?
What is't thou canst demand?

speeches.speech: My liberty.
lines: My liberty.

speeches.speech: Before the time be out? no more!
lines: Before the time be out? no more!

speeches.speech: I prithee, Remember I have done thee worthy service; Told thee no lies, made thee no mistakings, served Without or grudge or grumblings: thou didst promise To bate me a full year.
lines: I prithee,
Remember I have done thee worthy service;
Told thee no lies, made thee no mistakings, served
Without or grudge or grumblings: thou didst promise
To bate me a full year.

speeches.speech: Dost thou forget From what a torment I did free thee?
lines: Dost thou forget
From what a torment I did free thee?

speeches.speech: No.
lines: No.

speeches.speech: Thou dost, and think'st it much to tread the ooze Of the salt deep, To run upon the sharp wind of the north, To do me business in the veins o' the earth When it is baked with frost.
lines: Thou dost, and think'st it much to tread the ooze
Of the salt deep,
To run upon the sharp wind of the north,
To do me business in the veins o' the earth
When it is baked with frost.

speeches.speech: I do not, sir.
lines: I do not, sir.

speeches.speech: Thou liest, malignant thing! Hast thou forgot The foul witch Sycorax, who with age and envy Was grown into a hoop? hast thou forgot her?
lines: Thou liest, malignant thing! Hast thou forgot
The foul witch Sycorax, who with age and envy
Was grown into a hoop? hast thou forgot her?

speeches.speech: No, sir.
lines: No, sir.

speeches.speech: Thou hast. Where was she born? speak; tell me.
lines: Thou hast. Where was she born? speak; tell me.

speeches.speech: Sir, in Argier.
lines: Sir, in Argier.

speeches.speech: O, was she so? I must Once in a month recount what thou hast been, Which thou forget'st. This damn'd witch Sycorax, For mischiefs manifold and sorceries terrible To enter human hearing, from Argier, Thou know'st, was banish'd: for one thing she did They would not take her life. Is not this true?
lines: O, was she so? I must
Once in a month recount what thou hast been,
Which thou forget'st. This damn'd witch Sycorax,
For mischiefs manifold and sorceries terrible
To enter human hearing, from Argier,
Thou know'st, was banish'd: for one thing she did
They would not take her life. Is not this true?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir.
lines: Ay, sir.

speeches.speech: This blue-eyed hag was hither brought with child And here was left by the sailors. Thou, my slave, As thou report'st thyself, wast then her servant; And, for thou wast a spirit too delicate To act her earthy and abhorr'd commands, Refusing her grand hests, she did confine thee, By help of her more potent ministers And in her most unmitigable rage, Into a cloven pine; within which rift Imprison'd thou didst painfully remain A dozen years; within which space she died And left thee there; where thou didst vent thy groans As fast as mill-wheels strike. Then was this island-- Save for the son that she did litter here, A freckled whelp hag-born--not honour'd with A human shape.
lines: This blue-eyed hag was hither brought with child
And here was left by the sailors. Thou, my slave,
As thou report'st thyself, wast then her servant;
And, for thou wast a spirit too delicate
To act her earthy and abhorr'd commands,
Refusing her grand hests, she did confine thee,
By help of her more potent ministers
And in her most unmitigable rage,
Into a cloven pine; within which rift
Imprison'd thou didst painfully remain
A dozen years; within which space she died
And left thee there; where thou didst vent thy groans
As fast as mill-wheels strike. Then was this island--
Save for the son that she did litter here,
A freckled whelp hag-born--not honour'd with
A human shape.

speeches.speech: Yes, Caliban her son.
lines: Yes, Caliban her son.

speeches.speech: Dull thing, I say so; he, that Caliban Whom now I keep in service. Thou best know'st What torment I did find thee in; thy groans Did make wolves howl and penetrate the breasts Of ever angry bears: it was a torment To lay upon the damn'd, which Sycorax Could not again undo: it was mine art, When I arrived and heard thee, that made gape The pine and let thee out.
lines: Dull thing, I say so; he, that Caliban
Whom now I keep in service. Thou best know'st
What torment I did find thee in; thy groans
Did make wolves howl and penetrate the breasts
Of ever angry bears: it was a torment
To lay upon the damn'd, which Sycorax
Could not again undo: it was mine art,
When I arrived and heard thee, that made gape
The pine and let thee out.

speeches.speech: I thank thee, master.
lines: I thank thee, master.

speeches.speech: If thou more murmur'st, I will rend an oak And peg thee in his knotty entrails till Thou hast howl'd away twelve winters.
lines: If thou more murmur'st, I will rend an oak
And peg thee in his knotty entrails till
Thou hast howl'd away twelve winters.

speeches.speech: Pardon, master; I will be correspondent to command And do my spiriting gently.
lines: Pardon, master;
I will be correspondent to command
And do my spiriting gently.

speeches.speech: Do so, and after two days I will discharge thee.
lines: Do so, and after two days
I will discharge thee.

speeches.speech: That's my noble master! What shall I do? say what; what shall I do?
lines: That's my noble master!
What shall I do? say what; what shall I do?

speeches.speech: Go make thyself like a nymph o' the sea: be subject To no sight but thine and mine, invisible To every eyeball else. Go take this shape And hither come in't: go, hence with diligence! [Exit ARIEL] Awake, dear heart, awake! thou hast slept well; Awake!
lines: Go make thyself like a nymph o' the sea: be subject
To no sight but thine and mine, invisible
To every eyeball else. Go take this shape
And hither come in't: go, hence with diligence!
[Exit ARIEL]
Awake, dear heart, awake! thou hast slept well; Awake!

speeches.speech: The strangeness of your story put Heaviness in me.
lines: The strangeness of your story put
Heaviness in me.

speeches.speech: Shake it off. Come on; We'll visit Caliban my slave, who never Yields us kind answer.
lines: Shake it off. Come on;
We'll visit Caliban my slave, who never
Yields us kind answer.

speeches.speech: 'Tis a villain, sir, I do not love to look on.
lines: 'Tis a villain, sir,
I do not love to look on.

speeches.speech: But, as 'tis, We cannot miss him: he does make our fire, Fetch in our wood and serves in offices That profit us. What, ho! slave! Caliban! Thou earth, thou! speak.
lines: But, as 'tis,
We cannot miss him: he does make our fire,
Fetch in our wood and serves in offices
That profit us. What, ho! slave! Caliban!
Thou earth, thou! speak.

speeches.speech: [Within] There's wood enough within.
lines: [Within] There's wood enough within.

speeches.speech: Come forth, I say! there's other business for thee: Come, thou tortoise! when? [Re-enter ARIEL like a water-nymph] Fine apparition! My quaint Ariel, Hark in thine ear.
lines: Come forth, I say! there's other business for thee:
Come, thou tortoise! when?
[Re-enter ARIEL like a water-nymph]
Fine apparition! My quaint Ariel,
Hark in thine ear.

speeches.speech: My lord it shall be done.
lines: My lord it shall be done.

speeches.speech: Thou poisonous slave, got by the devil himself Upon thy wicked dam, come forth!
lines: Thou poisonous slave, got by the devil himself
Upon thy wicked dam, come forth!

speeches.speech: As wicked dew as e'er my mother brush'd With raven's feather from unwholesome fen Drop on you both! a south-west blow on ye And blister you all o'er!
lines: As wicked dew as e'er my mother brush'd
With raven's feather from unwholesome fen
Drop on you both! a south-west blow on ye
And blister you all o'er!

speeches.speech: For this, be sure, to-night thou shalt have cramps, Side-stitches that shall pen thy breath up; urchins Shall, for that vast of night that they may work, All exercise on thee; thou shalt be pinch'd As thick as honeycomb, each pinch more stinging Than bees that made 'em.
lines: For this, be sure, to-night thou shalt have cramps,
Side-stitches that shall pen thy breath up; urchins
Shall, for that vast of night that they may work,
All exercise on thee; thou shalt be pinch'd
As thick as honeycomb, each pinch more stinging
Than bees that made 'em.

speeches.speech: I must eat my dinner. This island's mine, by Sycorax my mother, Which thou takest from me. When thou camest first, Thou strokedst me and madest much of me, wouldst give me Water with berries in't, and teach me how To name the bigger light, and how the less, That burn by day and night: and then I loved thee And show'd thee all the qualities o' the isle, The fresh springs, brine-pits, barren place and fertile: Cursed be I that did so! All the charms Of Sycorax, toads, beetles, bats, light on you! For I am all the subjects that you have, Which first was mine own king: and here you sty me In this hard rock, whiles you do keep from me The rest o' the island.
lines: I must eat my dinner.
This island's mine, by Sycorax my mother,
Which thou takest from me. When thou camest first,
Thou strokedst me and madest much of me, wouldst give me
Water with berries in't, and teach me how
To name the bigger light, and how the less,
That burn by day and night: and then I loved thee
And show'd thee all the qualities o' the isle,
The fresh springs, brine-pits, barren place and fertile:
Cursed be I that did so! All the charms
Of Sycorax, toads, beetles, bats, light on you!
For I am all the subjects that you have,
Which first was mine own king: and here you sty me
In this hard rock, whiles you do keep from me
The rest o' the island.

speeches.speech: Thou most lying slave, Whom stripes may move, not kindness! I have used thee, Filth as thou art, with human care, and lodged thee In mine own cell, till thou didst seek to violate The honour of my child.
lines: Thou most lying slave,
Whom stripes may move, not kindness! I have used thee,
Filth as thou art, with human care, and lodged thee
In mine own cell, till thou didst seek to violate
The honour of my child.

speeches.speech: O ho, O ho! would't had been done! Thou didst prevent me; I had peopled else This isle with Calibans.
lines: O ho, O ho! would't had been done!
Thou didst prevent me; I had peopled else
This isle with Calibans.

speeches.speech: Abhorred slave, Which any print of goodness wilt not take, Being capable of all ill! I pitied thee, Took pains to make thee speak, taught thee each hour One thing or other: when thou didst not, savage, Know thine own meaning, but wouldst gabble like A thing most brutish, I endow'd thy purposes With words that made them known. But thy vile race, Though thou didst learn, had that in't which good natures Could not abide to be with; therefore wast thou Deservedly confined into this rock, Who hadst deserved more than a prison.
lines: Abhorred slave,
Which any print of goodness wilt not take,
Being capable of all ill! I pitied thee,
Took pains to make thee speak, taught thee each hour
One thing or other: when thou didst not, savage,
Know thine own meaning, but wouldst gabble like
A thing most brutish, I endow'd thy purposes
With words that made them known. But thy vile race,
Though thou didst learn, had that in't which
good natures
Could not abide to be with; therefore wast thou
Deservedly confined into this rock,
Who hadst deserved more than a prison.

speeches.speech: You taught me language; and my profit on't Is, I know how to curse. The red plague rid you For learning me your language!
lines: You taught me language; and my profit on't
Is, I know how to curse. The red plague rid you
For learning me your language!

speeches.speech: Hag-seed, hence! Fetch us in fuel; and be quick, thou'rt best, To answer other business. Shrug'st thou, malice? If thou neglect'st or dost unwillingly What I command, I'll rack thee with old cramps, Fill all thy bones with aches, make thee roar That beasts shall tremble at thy din.
lines: Hag-seed, hence!
Fetch us in fuel; and be quick, thou'rt best,
To answer other business. Shrug'st thou, malice?
If thou neglect'st or dost unwillingly
What I command, I'll rack thee with old cramps,
Fill all thy bones with aches, make thee roar
That beasts shall tremble at thy din.

speeches.speech: No, pray thee. [Aside] I must obey: his art is of such power, It would control my dam's god, Setebos, and make a vassal of him.
lines: No, pray thee.
[Aside]
I must obey: his art is of such power,
It would control my dam's god, Setebos,
and make a vassal of him.

speeches.speech: So, slave; hence! [Exit CALIBAN] [Re-enter ARIEL, invisible, playing and singing;] FERDINAND following] ARIEL'S song. Come unto these yellow sands, And then take hands: Courtsied when you have and kiss'd The wild waves whist, Foot it featly here and there; And, sweet sprites, the burthen bear. Hark, hark! [Burthen [dispersedly, within] Bow-wow] The watch-dogs bark! [Burthen Bow-wow] Hark, hark! I hear The strain of strutting chanticleer Cry, Cock-a-diddle-dow.
lines: So, slave; hence!
[Exit CALIBAN]
[Re-enter ARIEL, invisible, playing and singing;]
FERDINAND following]
ARIEL'S song.
Come unto these yellow sands,
And then take hands:
Courtsied when you have and kiss'd
The wild waves whist,
Foot it featly here and there;
And, sweet sprites, the burthen bear.
Hark, hark!
[Burthen [dispersedly, within] Bow-wow]
The watch-dogs bark!
[Burthen Bow-wow]
Hark, hark! I hear
The strain of strutting chanticleer
Cry, Cock-a-diddle-dow.

speeches.speech: Where should this music be? i' the air or the earth? It sounds no more: and sure, it waits upon Some god o' the island. Sitting on a bank, Weeping again the king my father's wreck, This music crept by me upon the waters, Allaying both their fury and my passion With its sweet air: thence I have follow'd it, Or it hath drawn me rather. But 'tis gone. No, it begins again. [ARIEL sings] Full fathom five thy father lies; Of his bones are coral made; Those are pearls that were his eyes: Nothing of him that doth fade But doth suffer a sea-change Into something rich and strange. Sea-nymphs hourly ring his knell [Burthen Ding-dong] Hark! now I hear them,--Ding-dong, bell.
lines: Where should this music be? i' the air or the earth?
It sounds no more: and sure, it waits upon
Some god o' the island. Sitting on a bank,
Weeping again the king my father's wreck,
This music crept by me upon the waters,
Allaying both their fury and my passion
With its sweet air: thence I have follow'd it,
Or it hath drawn me rather. But 'tis gone.
No, it begins again.
[ARIEL sings]
Full fathom five thy father lies;
Of his bones are coral made;
Those are pearls that were his eyes:
Nothing of him that doth fade
But doth suffer a sea-change
Into something rich and strange.
Sea-nymphs hourly ring his knell
[Burthen Ding-dong]
Hark! now I hear them,--Ding-dong, bell.

speeches.speech: The ditty does remember my drown'd father. This is no mortal business, nor no sound That the earth owes. I hear it now above me.
lines: The ditty does remember my drown'd father.
This is no mortal business, nor no sound
That the earth owes. I hear it now above me.

speeches.speech: The fringed curtains of thine eye advance And say what thou seest yond.
lines: The fringed curtains of thine eye advance
And say what thou seest yond.

speeches.speech: What is't? a spirit? Lord, how it looks about! Believe me, sir, It carries a brave form. But 'tis a spirit.
lines: What is't? a spirit?
Lord, how it looks about! Believe me, sir,
It carries a brave form. But 'tis a spirit.

speeches.speech: No, wench; it eats and sleeps and hath such senses As we have, such. This gallant which thou seest Was in the wreck; and, but he's something stain'd With grief that's beauty's canker, thou mightst call him A goodly person: he hath lost his fellows And strays about to find 'em.
lines: No, wench; it eats and sleeps and hath such senses
As we have, such. This gallant which thou seest
Was in the wreck; and, but he's something stain'd
With grief that's beauty's canker, thou mightst call him
A goodly person: he hath lost his fellows
And strays about to find 'em.

speeches.speech: I might call him A thing divine, for nothing natural I ever saw so noble.
lines: I might call him
A thing divine, for nothing natural
I ever saw so noble.

speeches.speech: [Aside] It goes on, I see, As my soul prompts it. Spirit, fine spirit! I'll free thee Within two days for this.
lines: [Aside] It goes on, I see,
As my soul prompts it. Spirit, fine spirit! I'll free thee
Within two days for this.

speeches.speech: Most sure, the goddess On whom these airs attend! Vouchsafe my prayer May know if you remain upon this island; And that you will some good instruction give How I may bear me here: my prime request, Which I do last pronounce, is, O you wonder! If you be maid or no?
lines: Most sure, the goddess
On whom these airs attend! Vouchsafe my prayer
May know if you remain upon this island;
And that you will some good instruction give
How I may bear me here: my prime request,
Which I do last pronounce, is, O you wonder!
If you be maid or no?

speeches.speech: No wonder, sir; But certainly a maid.
lines: No wonder, sir;
But certainly a maid.

speeches.speech: My language! heavens! I am the best of them that speak this speech, Were I but where 'tis spoken.
lines: My language! heavens!
I am the best of them that speak this speech,
Were I but where 'tis spoken.

speeches.speech: How? the best? What wert thou, if the King of Naples heard thee?
lines: How? the best?
What wert thou, if the King of Naples heard thee?

speeches.speech: A single thing, as I am now, that wonders To hear thee speak of Naples. He does hear me; And that he does I weep: myself am Naples, Who with mine eyes, never since at ebb, beheld The king my father wreck'd.
lines: A single thing, as I am now, that wonders
To hear thee speak of Naples. He does hear me;
And that he does I weep: myself am Naples,
Who with mine eyes, never since at ebb, beheld
The king my father wreck'd.

speeches.speech: Alack, for mercy!
lines: Alack, for mercy!

speeches.speech: Yes, faith, and all his lords; the Duke of Milan And his brave son being twain.
lines: Yes, faith, and all his lords; the Duke of Milan
And his brave son being twain.

speeches.speech: [Aside]. The Duke of Milan And his more braver daughter could control thee, If now 'twere fit to do't. At the first sight They have changed eyes. Delicate Ariel, I'll set thee free for this. [To FERDINAND] A word, good sir; I fear you have done yourself some wrong: a word.
lines: [Aside]. The Duke of Milan
And his more braver daughter could control thee,
If now 'twere fit to do't. At the first sight
They have changed eyes. Delicate Ariel,
I'll set thee free for this.
[To FERDINAND]
A word, good sir;
I fear you have done yourself some wrong: a word.

speeches.speech: Why speaks my father so ungently? This Is the third man that e'er I saw, the first That e'er I sigh'd for: pity move my father To be inclined my way!
lines: Why speaks my father so ungently? This
Is the third man that e'er I saw, the first
That e'er I sigh'd for: pity move my father
To be inclined my way!

speeches.speech: O, if a virgin, And your affection not gone forth, I'll make you The queen of Naples.
lines: O, if a virgin,
And your affection not gone forth, I'll make you
The queen of Naples.

speeches.speech: Soft, sir! one word more. [Aside] They are both in either's powers; but this swift business I must uneasy make, lest too light winning Make the prize light. [To FERDINAND] One word more; I charge thee That thou attend me: thou dost here usurp The name thou owest not; and hast put thyself Upon this island as a spy, to win it From me, the lord on't.
lines: Soft, sir! one word more.
[Aside]
They are both in either's powers; but this swift business
I must uneasy make, lest too light winning
Make the prize light.
[To FERDINAND]
One word more; I charge thee
That thou attend me: thou dost here usurp
The name thou owest not; and hast put thyself
Upon this island as a spy, to win it
From me, the lord on't.

speeches.speech: No, as I am a man.
lines: No, as I am a man.

speeches.speech: There's nothing ill can dwell in such a temple: If the ill spirit have so fair a house, Good things will strive to dwell with't.
lines: There's nothing ill can dwell in such a temple:
If the ill spirit have so fair a house,
Good things will strive to dwell with't.

speeches.speech: Follow me. Speak not you for him; he's a traitor. Come; I'll manacle thy neck and feet together: Sea-water shalt thou drink; thy food shall be The fresh-brook muscles, wither'd roots and husks Wherein the acorn cradled. Follow.
lines: Follow me.
Speak not you for him; he's a traitor. Come;
I'll manacle thy neck and feet together:
Sea-water shalt thou drink; thy food shall be
The fresh-brook muscles, wither'd roots and husks
Wherein the acorn cradled. Follow.

speeches.speech: No; I will resist such entertainment till Mine enemy has more power.
lines: No;
I will resist such entertainment till
Mine enemy has more power.

speeches.speech: O dear father, Make not too rash a trial of him, for He's gentle and not fearful.
lines: O dear father,
Make not too rash a trial of him, for
He's gentle and not fearful.

speeches.speech: What? I say, My foot my tutor? Put thy sword up, traitor; Who makest a show but darest not strike, thy conscience Is so possess'd with guilt: come from thy ward, For I can here disarm thee with this stick And make thy weapon drop.
lines: What? I say,
My foot my tutor? Put thy sword up, traitor;
Who makest a show but darest not strike, thy conscience
Is so possess'd with guilt: come from thy ward,
For I can here disarm thee with this stick
And make thy weapon drop.

speeches.speech: Beseech you, father.
lines: Beseech you, father.

speeches.speech: Hence! hang not on my garments.
lines: Hence! hang not on my garments.

speeches.speech: Sir, have pity; I'll be his surety.
lines: Sir, have pity;
I'll be his surety.

speeches.speech: Silence! one word more Shall make me chide thee, if not hate thee. What! An advocate for an imposter! hush! Thou think'st there is no more such shapes as he, Having seen but him and Caliban: foolish wench! To the most of men this is a Caliban And they to him are angels.
lines: Silence! one word more
Shall make me chide thee, if not hate thee. What!
An advocate for an imposter! hush!
Thou think'st there is no more such shapes as he,
Having seen but him and Caliban: foolish wench!
To the most of men this is a Caliban
And they to him are angels.

speeches.speech: My affections Are then most humble; I have no ambition To see a goodlier man.
lines: My affections
Are then most humble; I have no ambition
To see a goodlier man.

speeches.speech: Come on; obey: Thy nerves are in their infancy again And have no vigour in them.
lines: Come on; obey:
Thy nerves are in their infancy again
And have no vigour in them.

speeches.speech: So they are; My spirits, as in a dream, are all bound up. My father's loss, the weakness which I feel, The wreck of all my friends, nor this man's threats, To whom I am subdued, are but light to me, Might I but through my prison once a day Behold this maid: all corners else o' the earth Let liberty make use of; space enough Have I in such a prison.
lines: So they are;
My spirits, as in a dream, are all bound up.
My father's loss, the weakness which I feel,
The wreck of all my friends, nor this man's threats,
To whom I am subdued, are but light to me,
Might I but through my prison once a day
Behold this maid: all corners else o' the earth
Let liberty make use of; space enough
Have I in such a prison.

speeches.speech: [Aside] It works. [To FERDINAND] Come on. Thou hast done well, fine Ariel! [To FERDINAND] Follow me. [To ARIEL] Hark what thou else shalt do me.
lines: [Aside] It works.
[To FERDINAND]
Come on.
Thou hast done well, fine Ariel!
[To FERDINAND]
Follow me.
[To ARIEL]
Hark what thou else shalt do me.

speeches.speech: Be of comfort; My father's of a better nature, sir, Than he appears by speech: this is unwonted Which now came from him.
lines: Be of comfort;
My father's of a better nature, sir,
Than he appears by speech: this is unwonted
Which now came from him.

speeches.speech: Thou shalt be free As mountain winds: but then exactly do All points of my command.
lines: Thou shalt be free
As mountain winds: but then exactly do
All points of my command.

speeches.speech: To the syllable.
lines: To the syllable.

speeches.speech: Come, follow. Speak not for him.
lines: Come, follow. Speak not for him.

speeches.speech: Beseech you, sir, be merry; you have cause, So have we all, of joy; for our escape Is much beyond our loss. Our hint of woe Is common; every day some sailor's wife, The masters of some merchant and the merchant Have just our theme of woe; but for the miracle, I mean our preservation, few in millions Can speak like us: then wisely, good sir, weigh Our sorrow with our comfort.
lines: Beseech you, sir, be merry; you have cause,
So have we all, of joy; for our escape
Is much beyond our loss. Our hint of woe
Is common; every day some sailor's wife,
The masters of some merchant and the merchant
Have just our theme of woe; but for the miracle,
I mean our preservation, few in millions
Can speak like us: then wisely, good sir, weigh
Our sorrow with our comfort.

speeches.speech: Prithee, peace.
lines: Prithee, peace.

speeches.speech: He receives comfort like cold porridge.
lines: He receives comfort like cold porridge.

speeches.speech: The visitor will not give him o'er so.
lines: The visitor will not give him o'er so.

speeches.speech: Look he's winding up the watch of his wit; by and by it will strike.
lines: Look he's winding up the watch of his wit;
by and by it will strike.

speeches.speech: Sir,--
lines: Sir,--

speeches.speech: One: tell.
lines: One: tell.

speeches.speech: When every grief is entertain'd that's offer'd, Comes to the entertainer--
lines: When every grief is entertain'd that's offer'd,
Comes to the entertainer--

speeches.speech: A dollar.
lines: A dollar.

speeches.speech: Dolour comes to him, indeed: you have spoken truer than you purposed.
lines: Dolour comes to him, indeed: you
have spoken truer than you purposed.

speeches.speech: You have taken it wiselier than I meant you should.
lines: You have taken it wiselier than I meant you should.

speeches.speech: Therefore, my lord,--
lines: Therefore, my lord,--

speeches.speech: Fie, what a spendthrift is he of his tongue!
lines: Fie, what a spendthrift is he of his tongue!

speeches.speech: I prithee, spare.
lines: I prithee, spare.

speeches.speech: Well, I have done: but yet,--
lines: Well, I have done: but yet,--

speeches.speech: He will be talking.
lines: He will be talking.

speeches.speech: Which, of he or Adrian, for a good wager, first begins to crow?
lines: Which, of he or Adrian, for a good
wager, first begins to crow?

speeches.speech: The old cock.
lines: The old cock.

speeches.speech: The cockerel.
lines: The cockerel.

speeches.speech: Done. The wager?
lines: Done. The wager?

speeches.speech: A laughter.
lines: A laughter.

speeches.speech: A match!
lines: A match!

speeches.speech: Though this island seem to be desert,--
lines: Though this island seem to be desert,--

speeches.speech: Ha, ha, ha! So, you're paid.
lines: Ha, ha, ha! So, you're paid.

speeches.speech: Uninhabitable and almost inaccessible,--
lines: Uninhabitable and almost inaccessible,--

speeches.speech: Yet,--
lines: Yet,--

speeches.speech: Yet,--
lines: Yet,--

speeches.speech: He could not miss't.
lines: He could not miss't.

speeches.speech: It must needs be of subtle, tender and delicate temperance.
lines: It must needs be of subtle, tender and delicate
temperance.

speeches.speech: Temperance was a delicate wench.
lines: Temperance was a delicate wench.

speeches.speech: Ay, and a subtle; as he most learnedly delivered.
lines: Ay, and a subtle; as he most learnedly delivered.

speeches.speech: The air breathes upon us here most sweetly.
lines: The air breathes upon us here most sweetly.

speeches.speech: As if it had lungs and rotten ones.
lines: As if it had lungs and rotten ones.

speeches.speech: Or as 'twere perfumed by a fen.
lines: Or as 'twere perfumed by a fen.

speeches.speech: Here is everything advantageous to life.
lines: Here is everything advantageous to life.

speeches.speech: True; save means to live.
lines: True; save means to live.

speeches.speech: Of that there's none, or little.
lines: Of that there's none, or little.

speeches.speech: How lush and lusty the grass looks! how green!
lines: How lush and lusty the grass looks! how green!

speeches.speech: The ground indeed is tawny.
lines: The ground indeed is tawny.

speeches.speech: With an eye of green in't.
lines: With an eye of green in't.

speeches.speech: He misses not much.
lines: He misses not much.

speeches.speech: No; he doth but mistake the truth totally.
lines: No; he doth but mistake the truth totally.

speeches.speech: But the rarity of it is,--which is indeed almost beyond credit,--
lines: But the rarity of it is,--which is indeed almost
beyond credit,--

speeches.speech: As many vouched rarities are.
lines: As many vouched rarities are.

speeches.speech: That our garments, being, as they were, drenched in the sea, hold notwithstanding their freshness and glosses, being rather new-dyed than stained with salt water.
lines: That our garments, being, as they were, drenched in
the sea, hold notwithstanding their freshness and
glosses, being rather new-dyed than stained with
salt water.

speeches.speech: If but one of his pockets could speak, would it not say he lies?
lines: If but one of his pockets could speak, would it not
say he lies?

speeches.speech: Ay, or very falsely pocket up his report
lines: Ay, or very falsely pocket up his report

speeches.speech: Methinks our garments are now as fresh as when we put them on first in Afric, at the marriage of the king's fair daughter Claribel to the King of Tunis.
lines: Methinks our garments are now as fresh as when we
put them on first in Afric, at the marriage of
the king's fair daughter Claribel to the King of Tunis.

speeches.speech: 'Twas a sweet marriage, and we prosper well in our return.
lines: 'Twas a sweet marriage, and we prosper well in our return.

speeches.speech: Tunis was never graced before with such a paragon to their queen.
lines: Tunis was never graced before with such a paragon to
their queen.

speeches.speech: Not since widow Dido's time.
lines: Not since widow Dido's time.

speeches.speech: Widow! a pox o' that! How came that widow in? widow Dido!
lines: Widow! a pox o' that! How came that widow in?
widow Dido!

speeches.speech: What if he had said 'widower AEneas' too? Good Lord, how you take it!
lines: What if he had said 'widower AEneas' too? Good Lord,
how you take it!

speeches.speech: 'Widow Dido' said you? you make me study of that: she was of Carthage, not of Tunis.
lines: 'Widow Dido' said you? you make me study of that:
she was of Carthage, not of Tunis.

speeches.speech: This Tunis, sir, was Carthage.
lines: This Tunis, sir, was Carthage.

speeches.speech: Carthage?
lines: Carthage?

speeches.speech: I assure you, Carthage.
lines: I assure you, Carthage.

speeches.speech: His word is more than the miraculous harp; he hath raised the wall and houses too.
lines: His word is more than the miraculous harp; he hath
raised the wall and houses too.

speeches.speech: What impossible matter will he make easy next?
lines: What impossible matter will he make easy next?

speeches.speech: I think he will carry this island home in his pocket and give it his son for an apple.
lines: I think he will carry this island home in his pocket
and give it his son for an apple.

speeches.speech: And, sowing the kernels of it in the sea, bring forth more islands.
lines: And, sowing the kernels of it in the sea, bring
forth more islands.

speeches.speech: Ay.
lines: Ay.

speeches.speech: Why, in good time.
lines: Why, in good time.

speeches.speech: Sir, we were talking that our garments seem now as fresh as when we were at Tunis at the marriage of your daughter, who is now queen.
lines: Sir, we were talking that our garments seem now
as fresh as when we were at Tunis at the marriage
of your daughter, who is now queen.

speeches.speech: And the rarest that e'er came there.
lines: And the rarest that e'er came there.

speeches.speech: Bate, I beseech you, widow Dido.
lines: Bate, I beseech you, widow Dido.

speeches.speech: O, widow Dido! ay, widow Dido.
lines: O, widow Dido! ay, widow Dido.

speeches.speech: Is not, sir, my doublet as fresh as the first day I wore it? I mean, in a sort.
lines: Is not, sir, my doublet as fresh as the first day I
wore it? I mean, in a sort.

speeches.speech: That sort was well fished for.
lines: That sort was well fished for.

speeches.speech: When I wore it at your daughter's marriage?
lines: When I wore it at your daughter's marriage?

speeches.speech: You cram these words into mine ears against The stomach of my sense. Would I had never Married my daughter there! for, coming thence, My son is lost and, in my rate, she too, Who is so far from Italy removed I ne'er again shall see her. O thou mine heir Of Naples and of Milan, what strange fish Hath made his meal on thee?
lines: You cram these words into mine ears against
The stomach of my sense. Would I had never
Married my daughter there! for, coming thence,
My son is lost and, in my rate, she too,
Who is so far from Italy removed
I ne'er again shall see her. O thou mine heir
Of Naples and of Milan, what strange fish
Hath made his meal on thee?

speeches.speech: Sir, he may live: I saw him beat the surges under him, And ride upon their backs; he trod the water, Whose enmity he flung aside, and breasted The surge most swoln that met him; his bold head 'Bove the contentious waves he kept, and oar'd Himself with his good arms in lusty stroke To the shore, that o'er his wave-worn basis bow'd, As stooping to relieve him: I not doubt He came alive to land.
lines: Sir, he may live:
I saw him beat the surges under him,
And ride upon their backs; he trod the water,
Whose enmity he flung aside, and breasted
The surge most swoln that met him; his bold head
'Bove the contentious waves he kept, and oar'd
Himself with his good arms in lusty stroke
To the shore, that o'er his wave-worn basis bow'd,
As stooping to relieve him: I not doubt
He came alive to land.

speeches.speech: No, no, he's gone.
lines: No, no, he's gone.

speeches.speech: Sir, you may thank yourself for this great loss, That would not bless our Europe with your daughter, But rather lose her to an African; Where she at least is banish'd from your eye, Who hath cause to wet the grief on't.
lines: Sir, you may thank yourself for this great loss,
That would not bless our Europe with your daughter,
But rather lose her to an African;
Where she at least is banish'd from your eye,
Who hath cause to wet the grief on't.

speeches.speech: Prithee, peace.
lines: Prithee, peace.

speeches.speech: You were kneel'd to and importuned otherwise By all of us, and the fair soul herself Weigh'd between loathness and obedience, at Which end o' the beam should bow. We have lost your son, I fear, for ever: Milan and Naples have More widows in them of this business' making Than we bring men to comfort them: The fault's your own.
lines: You were kneel'd to and importuned otherwise
By all of us, and the fair soul herself
Weigh'd between loathness and obedience, at
Which end o' the beam should bow. We have lost your
son,
I fear, for ever: Milan and Naples have
More widows in them of this business' making
Than we bring men to comfort them:
The fault's your own.

speeches.speech: So is the dear'st o' the loss.
lines: So is the dear'st o' the loss.

speeches.speech: My lord Sebastian, The truth you speak doth lack some gentleness And time to speak it in: you rub the sore, When you should bring the plaster.
lines: My lord Sebastian,
The truth you speak doth lack some gentleness
And time to speak it in: you rub the sore,
When you should bring the plaster.

speeches.speech: Very well.
lines: Very well.

speeches.speech: And most chirurgeonly.
lines: And most chirurgeonly.

speeches.speech: It is foul weather in us all, good sir, When you are cloudy.
lines: It is foul weather in us all, good sir,
When you are cloudy.

speeches.speech: Foul weather?
lines: Foul weather?

speeches.speech: Very foul.
lines: Very foul.

speeches.speech: Had I plantation of this isle, my lord,--
lines: Had I plantation of this isle, my lord,--

speeches.speech: He'ld sow't with nettle-seed.
lines: He'ld sow't with nettle-seed.

speeches.speech: Or docks, or mallows.
lines: Or docks, or mallows.

speeches.speech: And were the king on't, what would I do?
lines: And were the king on't, what would I do?

speeches.speech: 'Scape being drunk for want of wine.
lines: 'Scape being drunk for want of wine.

speeches.speech: I' the commonwealth I would by contraries Execute all things; for no kind of traffic Would I admit; no name of magistrate; Letters should not be known; riches, poverty, And use of service, none; contract, succession, Bourn, bound of land, tilth, vineyard, none; No use of metal, corn, or wine, or oil; No occupation; all men idle, all; And women too, but innocent and pure; No sovereignty;--
lines: I' the commonwealth I would by contraries
Execute all things; for no kind of traffic
Would I admit; no name of magistrate;
Letters should not be known; riches, poverty,
And use of service, none; contract, succession,
Bourn, bound of land, tilth, vineyard, none;
No use of metal, corn, or wine, or oil;
No occupation; all men idle, all;
And women too, but innocent and pure;
No sovereignty;--

speeches.speech: Yet he would be king on't.
lines: Yet he would be king on't.

speeches.speech: The latter end of his commonwealth forgets the beginning.
lines: The latter end of his commonwealth forgets the
beginning.

speeches.speech: All things in common nature should produce Without sweat or endeavour: treason, felony, Sword, pike, knife, gun, or need of any engine, Would I not have; but nature should bring forth, Of its own kind, all foison, all abundance, To feed my innocent people.
lines: All things in common nature should produce
Without sweat or endeavour: treason, felony,
Sword, pike, knife, gun, or need of any engine,
Would I not have; but nature should bring forth,
Of its own kind, all foison, all abundance,
To feed my innocent people.

speeches.speech: No marrying 'mong his subjects?
lines: No marrying 'mong his subjects?

speeches.speech: None, man; all idle: whores and knaves.
lines: None, man; all idle: whores and knaves.

speeches.speech: I would with such perfection govern, sir, To excel the golden age.
lines: I would with such perfection govern, sir,
To excel the golden age.

speeches.speech: God save his majesty!
lines: God save his majesty!

speeches.speech: Long live Gonzalo!
lines: Long live Gonzalo!

speeches.speech: And,--do you mark me, sir?
lines: And,--do you mark me, sir?

speeches.speech: Prithee, no more: thou dost talk nothing to me.
lines: Prithee, no more: thou dost talk nothing to me.

speeches.speech: I do well believe your highness; and did it to minister occasion to these gentlemen, who are of such sensible and nimble lungs that they always use to laugh at nothing.
lines: I do well believe your highness; and
did it to minister occasion to these gentlemen,
who are of such sensible and nimble lungs that
they always use to laugh at nothing.

speeches.speech: 'Twas you we laughed at.
lines: 'Twas you we laughed at.

speeches.speech: Who in this kind of merry fooling am nothing to you: so you may continue and laugh at nothing still.
lines: Who in this kind of merry fooling am nothing
to you: so you may continue and laugh at
nothing still.

speeches.speech: What a blow was there given!
lines: What a blow was there given!

speeches.speech: An it had not fallen flat-long.
lines: An it had not fallen flat-long.

speeches.speech: You are gentlemen of brave metal; you would lift the moon out of her sphere, if she would continue in it five weeks without changing.
lines: You are gentlemen of brave metal; you would lift
the moon out of her sphere, if she would continue
in it five weeks without changing.

speeches.speech: We would so, and then go a bat-fowling.
lines: We would so, and then go a bat-fowling.

speeches.speech: Nay, good my lord, be not angry.
lines: Nay, good my lord, be not angry.

speeches.speech: No, I warrant you; I will not adventure my discretion so weakly. Will you laugh me asleep, for I am very heavy?
lines: No, I warrant you; I will not adventure
my discretion so weakly. Will you laugh
me asleep, for I am very heavy?

speeches.speech: Go sleep, and hear us.
lines: Go sleep, and hear us.

speeches.speech: What, all so soon asleep! I wish mine eyes Would, with themselves, shut up my thoughts: I find They are inclined to do so.
lines: What, all so soon asleep! I wish mine eyes
Would, with themselves, shut up my thoughts: I find
They are inclined to do so.

speeches.speech: Please you, sir, Do not omit the heavy offer of it: It seldom visits sorrow; when it doth, It is a comforter.
lines: Please you, sir,
Do not omit the heavy offer of it:
It seldom visits sorrow; when it doth,
It is a comforter.

speeches.speech: We two, my lord, Will guard your person while you take your rest, And watch your safety.
lines: We two, my lord,
Will guard your person while you take your rest,
And watch your safety.

speeches.speech: Thank you. Wondrous heavy.
lines: Thank you. Wondrous heavy.

speeches.speech: What a strange drowsiness possesses them!
lines: What a strange drowsiness possesses them!

speeches.speech: It is the quality o' the climate.
lines: It is the quality o' the climate.

speeches.speech: Why Doth it not then our eyelids sink? I find not Myself disposed to sleep.
lines: Why
Doth it not then our eyelids sink? I find not
Myself disposed to sleep.

speeches.speech: Nor I; my spirits are nimble. They fell together all, as by consent; They dropp'd, as by a thunder-stroke. What might, Worthy Sebastian? O, what might?--No more:-- And yet me thinks I see it in thy face, What thou shouldst be: the occasion speaks thee, and My strong imagination sees a crown Dropping upon thy head.
lines: Nor I; my spirits are nimble.
They fell together all, as by consent;
They dropp'd, as by a thunder-stroke. What might,
Worthy Sebastian? O, what might?--No more:--
And yet me thinks I see it in thy face,
What thou shouldst be: the occasion speaks thee, and
My strong imagination sees a crown
Dropping upon thy head.

speeches.speech: What, art thou waking?
lines: What, art thou waking?

speeches.speech: Do you not hear me speak?
lines: Do you not hear me speak?

speeches.speech: I do; and surely It is a sleepy language and thou speak'st Out of thy sleep. What is it thou didst say? This is a strange repose, to be asleep With eyes wide open; standing, speaking, moving, And yet so fast asleep.
lines: I do; and surely
It is a sleepy language and thou speak'st
Out of thy sleep. What is it thou didst say?
This is a strange repose, to be asleep
With eyes wide open; standing, speaking, moving,
And yet so fast asleep.

speeches.speech: Noble Sebastian, Thou let'st thy fortune sleep--die, rather; wink'st Whiles thou art waking.
lines: Noble Sebastian,
Thou let'st thy fortune sleep--die, rather; wink'st
Whiles thou art waking.

speeches.speech: Thou dost snore distinctly; There's meaning in thy snores.
lines: Thou dost snore distinctly;
There's meaning in thy snores.

speeches.speech: I am more serious than my custom: you Must be so too, if heed me; which to do Trebles thee o'er.
lines: I am more serious than my custom: you
Must be so too, if heed me; which to do
Trebles thee o'er.

speeches.speech: Well, I am standing water.
lines: Well, I am standing water.

speeches.speech: I'll teach you how to flow.
lines: I'll teach you how to flow.

speeches.speech: Do so: to ebb Hereditary sloth instructs me.
lines: Do so: to ebb
Hereditary sloth instructs me.

speeches.speech: O, If you but knew how you the purpose cherish Whiles thus you mock it! how, in stripping it, You more invest it! Ebbing men, indeed, Most often do so near the bottom run By their own fear or sloth.
lines: O,
If you but knew how you the purpose cherish
Whiles thus you mock it! how, in stripping it,
You more invest it! Ebbing men, indeed,
Most often do so near the bottom run
By their own fear or sloth.

speeches.speech: Prithee, say on: The setting of thine eye and cheek proclaim A matter from thee, and a birth indeed Which throes thee much to yield.
lines: Prithee, say on:
The setting of thine eye and cheek proclaim
A matter from thee, and a birth indeed
Which throes thee much to yield.

speeches.speech: Thus, sir: Although this lord of weak remembrance, this, Who shall be of as little memory When he is earth'd, hath here almost persuade,-- For he's a spirit of persuasion, only Professes to persuade,--the king his son's alive, 'Tis as impossible that he's undrown'd And he that sleeps here swims.
lines: Thus, sir:
Although this lord of weak remembrance, this,
Who shall be of as little memory
When he is earth'd, hath here almost persuade,--
For he's a spirit of persuasion, only
Professes to persuade,--the king his son's alive,
'Tis as impossible that he's undrown'd
And he that sleeps here swims.

speeches.speech: I have no hope That he's undrown'd.
lines: I have no hope
That he's undrown'd.

speeches.speech: O, out of that 'no hope' What great hope have you! no hope that way is Another way so high a hope that even Ambition cannot pierce a wink beyond, But doubt discovery there. Will you grant with me That Ferdinand is drown'd?
lines: O, out of that 'no hope'
What great hope have you! no hope that way is
Another way so high a hope that even
Ambition cannot pierce a wink beyond,
But doubt discovery there. Will you grant with me
That Ferdinand is drown'd?

speeches.speech: He's gone.
lines: He's gone.

speeches.speech: Then, tell me, Who's the next heir of Naples?
lines: Then, tell me,
Who's the next heir of Naples?

speeches.speech: Claribel.
lines: Claribel.

speeches.speech: She that is queen of Tunis; she that dwells Ten leagues beyond man's life; she that from Naples Can have no note, unless the sun were post-- The man i' the moon's too slow--till new-born chins Be rough and razorable; she that--from whom? We all were sea-swallow'd, though some cast again, And by that destiny to perform an act Whereof what's past is prologue, what to come In yours and my discharge.
lines: She that is queen of Tunis; she that dwells
Ten leagues beyond man's life; she that from Naples
Can have no note, unless the sun were post--
The man i' the moon's too slow--till new-born chins
Be rough and razorable; she that--from whom?
We all were sea-swallow'd, though some cast again,
And by that destiny to perform an act
Whereof what's past is prologue, what to come
In yours and my discharge.

speeches.speech: What stuff is this! how say you? 'Tis true, my brother's daughter's queen of Tunis; So is she heir of Naples; 'twixt which regions There is some space.
lines: What stuff is this! how say you?
'Tis true, my brother's daughter's queen of Tunis;
So is she heir of Naples; 'twixt which regions
There is some space.

speeches.speech: A space whose every cubit Seems to cry out, 'How shall that Claribel Measure us back to Naples? Keep in Tunis, And let Sebastian wake.' Say, this were death That now hath seized them; why, they were no worse Than now they are. There be that can rule Naples As well as he that sleeps; lords that can prate As amply and unnecessarily As this Gonzalo; I myself could make A chough of as deep chat. O, that you bore The mind that I do! what a sleep were this For your advancement! Do you understand me?
lines: A space whose every cubit
Seems to cry out, 'How shall that Claribel
Measure us back to Naples? Keep in Tunis,
And let Sebastian wake.' Say, this were death
That now hath seized them; why, they were no worse
Than now they are. There be that can rule Naples
As well as he that sleeps; lords that can prate
As amply and unnecessarily
As this Gonzalo; I myself could make
A chough of as deep chat. O, that you bore
The mind that I do! what a sleep were this
For your advancement! Do you understand me?

speeches.speech: Methinks I do.
lines: Methinks I do.

speeches.speech: And how does your content Tender your own good fortune?
lines: And how does your content
Tender your own good fortune?

speeches.speech: I remember You did supplant your brother Prospero.
lines: I remember
You did supplant your brother Prospero.

speeches.speech: True: And look how well my garments sit upon me; Much feater than before: my brother's servants Were then my fellows; now they are my men.
lines: True:
And look how well my garments sit upon me;
Much feater than before: my brother's servants
Were then my fellows; now they are my men.

speeches.speech: But, for your conscience?
lines: But, for your conscience?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir; where lies that? if 'twere a kibe, 'Twould put me to my slipper: but I feel not This deity in my bosom: twenty consciences, That stand 'twixt me and Milan, candied be they And melt ere they molest! Here lies your brother, No better than the earth he lies upon, If he were that which now he's like, that's dead; Whom I, with this obedient steel, three inches of it, Can lay to bed for ever; whiles you, doing thus, To the perpetual wink for aye might put This ancient morsel, this Sir Prudence, who Should not upbraid our course. For all the rest, They'll take suggestion as a cat laps milk; They'll tell the clock to any business that We say befits the hour.
lines: Ay, sir; where lies that? if 'twere a kibe,
'Twould put me to my slipper: but I feel not
This deity in my bosom: twenty consciences,
That stand 'twixt me and Milan, candied be they
And melt ere they molest! Here lies your brother,
No better than the earth he lies upon,
If he were that which now he's like, that's dead;
Whom I, with this obedient steel, three inches of it,
Can lay to bed for ever; whiles you, doing thus,
To the perpetual wink for aye might put
This ancient morsel, this Sir Prudence, who
Should not upbraid our course. For all the rest,
They'll take suggestion as a cat laps milk;
They'll tell the clock to any business that
