We say befits the hour.

speeches.speech: Thy case, dear friend, Shall be my precedent; as thou got'st Milan, I'll come by Naples. Draw thy sword: one stroke Shall free thee from the tribute which thou payest; And I the king shall love thee.
lines: Thy case, dear friend,
Shall be my precedent; as thou got'st Milan,
I'll come by Naples. Draw thy sword: one stroke
Shall free thee from the tribute which thou payest;
And I the king shall love thee.

speeches.speech: Draw together; And when I rear my hand, do you the like, To fall it on Gonzalo.
lines: Draw together;
And when I rear my hand, do you the like,
To fall it on Gonzalo.

speeches.speech: O, but one word.
lines: O, but one word.

speeches.speech: My master through his art foresees the danger That you, his friend, are in; and sends me forth-- For else his project dies--to keep them living. [Sings in GONZALO's ear] While you here do snoring lie, Open-eyed conspiracy His time doth take. If of life you keep a care, Shake off slumber, and beware: Awake, awake!
lines: My master through his art foresees the danger
That you, his friend, are in; and sends me forth--
For else his project dies--to keep them living.
[Sings in GONZALO's ear]
While you here do snoring lie,
Open-eyed conspiracy
His time doth take.
If of life you keep a care,
Shake off slumber, and beware:
Awake, awake!

speeches.speech: Then let us both be sudden.
lines: Then let us both be sudden.

speeches.speech: Now, good angels Preserve the king.
lines: Now, good angels
Preserve the king.

speeches.speech: Why, how now? ho, awake! Why are you drawn? Wherefore this ghastly looking?
lines: Why, how now? ho, awake! Why are you drawn?
Wherefore this ghastly looking?

speeches.speech: What's the matter?
lines: What's the matter?

speeches.speech: Whiles we stood here securing your repose, Even now, we heard a hollow burst of bellowing Like bulls, or rather lions: did't not wake you? It struck mine ear most terribly.
lines: Whiles we stood here securing your repose,
Even now, we heard a hollow burst of bellowing
Like bulls, or rather lions: did't not wake you?
It struck mine ear most terribly.

speeches.speech: I heard nothing.
lines: I heard nothing.

speeches.speech: O, 'twas a din to fright a monster's ear, To make an earthquake! sure, it was the roar Of a whole herd of lions.
lines: O, 'twas a din to fright a monster's ear,
To make an earthquake! sure, it was the roar
Of a whole herd of lions.

speeches.speech: Heard you this, Gonzalo?
lines: Heard you this, Gonzalo?

speeches.speech: Upon mine honour, sir, I heard a humming, And that a strange one too, which did awake me: I shaked you, sir, and cried: as mine eyes open'd, I saw their weapons drawn: there was a noise, That's verily. 'Tis best we stand upon our guard, Or that we quit this place; let's draw our weapons.
lines: Upon mine honour, sir, I heard a humming,
And that a strange one too, which did awake me:
I shaked you, sir, and cried: as mine eyes open'd,
I saw their weapons drawn: there was a noise,
That's verily. 'Tis best we stand upon our guard,
Or that we quit this place; let's draw our weapons.

speeches.speech: Lead off this ground; and let's make further search For my poor son.
lines: Lead off this ground; and let's make further search
For my poor son.

speeches.speech: Heavens keep him from these beasts! For he is, sure, i' the island.
lines: Heavens keep him from these beasts!
For he is, sure, i' the island.

speeches.speech: Lead away.
lines: Lead away.

speeches.speech: Prospero my lord shall know what I have done: So, king, go safely on to seek thy son.
lines: Prospero my lord shall know what I have done:
So, king, go safely on to seek thy son.

speeches.speech: All the infections that the sun sucks up From bogs, fens, flats, on Prosper fall and make him By inch-meal a disease! His spirits hear me And yet I needs must curse. But they'll nor pinch, Fright me with urchin--shows, pitch me i' the mire, Nor lead me, like a firebrand, in the dark Out of my way, unless he bid 'em; but For every trifle are they set upon me; Sometime like apes that mow and chatter at me And after bite me, then like hedgehogs which Lie tumbling in my barefoot way and mount Their pricks at my footfall; sometime am I All wound with adders who with cloven tongues Do hiss me into madness. [Enter TRINCULO] Lo, now, lo! Here comes a spirit of his, and to torment me For bringing wood in slowly. I'll fall flat; Perchance he will not mind me.
lines: All the infections that the sun sucks up
From bogs, fens, flats, on Prosper fall and make him
By inch-meal a disease! His spirits hear me
And yet I needs must curse. But they'll nor pinch,
Fright me with urchin--shows, pitch me i' the mire,
Nor lead me, like a firebrand, in the dark
Out of my way, unless he bid 'em; but
For every trifle are they set upon me;
Sometime like apes that mow and chatter at me
And after bite me, then like hedgehogs which
Lie tumbling in my barefoot way and mount
Their pricks at my footfall; sometime am I
All wound with adders who with cloven tongues
Do hiss me into madness.
[Enter TRINCULO]
Lo, now, lo!
Here comes a spirit of his, and to torment me
For bringing wood in slowly. I'll fall flat;
Perchance he will not mind me.

speeches.speech: Here's neither bush nor shrub, to bear off any weather at all, and another storm brewing; I hear it sing i' the wind: yond same black cloud, yond huge one, looks like a foul bombard that would shed his liquor. If it should thunder as it did before, I know not where to hide my head: yond same cloud cannot choose but fall by pailfuls. What have we here? a man or a fish? dead or alive? A fish: he smells like a fish; a very ancient and fish- like smell; a kind of not of the newest Poor- John. A strange fish! Were I in England now, as once I was, and had but this fish painted, not a holiday fool there but would give a piece of silver: there would this monster make a man; any strange beast there makes a man: when they will not give a doit to relieve a lame beggar, they will lazy out ten to see a dead Indian. Legged like a man and his fins like arms! Warm o' my troth! I do now let loose my opinion; hold it no longer: this is no fish, but an islander, that hath lately suffered by a thunderbolt. [Thunder] Alas, the storm is come again! my best way is to creep under his gaberdine; there is no other shelter hereabouts: misery acquaints a man with strange bed-fellows. I will here shroud till the dregs of the storm be past.
lines: Here's neither bush nor shrub, to bear off
any weather at all, and another storm brewing;
I hear it sing i' the wind: yond same black
cloud, yond huge one, looks like a foul
bombard that would shed his liquor. If it
should thunder as it did before, I know not
where to hide my head: yond same cloud cannot
choose but fall by pailfuls. What have we
here? a man or a fish? dead or alive? A fish:
he smells like a fish; a very ancient and fish-
like smell; a kind of not of the newest Poor-
John. A strange fish! Were I in England now,
as once I was, and had but this fish painted,
not a holiday fool there but would give a piece
of silver: there would this monster make a
man; any strange beast there makes a man:
when they will not give a doit to relieve a lame
beggar, they will lazy out ten to see a dead
Indian. Legged like a man and his fins like
arms! Warm o' my troth! I do now let loose
my opinion; hold it no longer: this is no fish,
but an islander, that hath lately suffered by a
thunderbolt.
[Thunder]
Alas, the storm is come again! my best way is to
creep under his gaberdine; there is no other
shelter hereabouts: misery acquaints a man with
strange bed-fellows. I will here shroud till the
dregs of the storm be past.

speeches.speech: I shall no more to sea, to sea, Here shall I die ashore-- This is a very scurvy tune to sing at a man's funeral: well, here's my comfort. [Drinks] [Sings] The master, the swabber, the boatswain and I, The gunner and his mate Loved Mall, Meg and Marian and Margery, But none of us cared for Kate; For she had a tongue with a tang, Would cry to a sailor, Go hang! She loved not the savour of tar nor of pitch, Yet a tailor might scratch her where'er she did itch: Then to sea, boys, and let her go hang! This is a scurvy tune too: but here's my comfort.
lines: I shall no more to sea, to sea,
Here shall I die ashore--
This is a very scurvy tune to sing at a man's
funeral: well, here's my comfort. [Drinks]
[Sings]
The master, the swabber, the boatswain and I,
The gunner and his mate
Loved Mall, Meg and Marian and Margery,
But none of us cared for Kate;
For she had a tongue with a tang,
Would cry to a sailor, Go hang!
She loved not the savour of tar nor of pitch,
Yet a tailor might scratch her where'er she did itch:
Then to sea, boys, and let her go hang!
This is a scurvy tune too: but here's my comfort.

speeches.speech: Do not torment me: Oh!
lines: Do not torment me: Oh!

speeches.speech: What's the matter? Have we devils here? Do you put tricks upon's with savages and men of Ind, ha? I have not scaped drowning to be afeard now of your four legs; for it hath been said, As proper a man as ever went on four legs cannot make him give ground; and it shall be said so again while Stephano breathes at's nostrils.
lines: What's the matter? Have we devils here? Do you put
tricks upon's with savages and men of Ind, ha? I
have not scaped drowning to be afeard now of your
four legs; for it hath been said, As proper a man as
ever went on four legs cannot make him give ground;
and it shall be said so again while Stephano
breathes at's nostrils.

speeches.speech: The spirit torments me; Oh!
lines: The spirit torments me; Oh!

speeches.speech: This is some monster of the isle with four legs, who hath got, as I take it, an ague. Where the devil should he learn our language? I will give him some relief, if it be but for that. if I can recover him and keep him tame and get to Naples with him, he's a present for any emperor that ever trod on neat's leather.
lines: This is some monster of the isle with four legs, who
hath got, as I take it, an ague. Where the devil
should he learn our language? I will give him some
relief, if it be but for that. if I can recover him
and keep him tame and get to Naples with him, he's a
present for any emperor that ever trod on neat's leather.

speeches.speech: Do not torment me, prithee; I'll bring my wood home faster.
lines: Do not torment me, prithee; I'll bring my wood home faster.

speeches.speech: He's in his fit now and does not talk after the wisest. He shall taste of my bottle: if he have never drunk wine afore will go near to remove his fit. If I can recover him and keep him tame, I will not take too much for him; he shall pay for him that hath him, and that soundly.
lines: He's in his fit now and does not talk after the
wisest. He shall taste of my bottle: if he have
never drunk wine afore will go near to remove his
fit. If I can recover him and keep him tame, I will
not take too much for him; he shall pay for him that
hath him, and that soundly.

speeches.speech: Thou dost me yet but little hurt; thou wilt anon, I know it by thy trembling: now Prosper works upon thee.
lines: Thou dost me yet but little hurt; thou wilt anon, I
know it by thy trembling: now Prosper works upon thee.

speeches.speech: Come on your ways; open your mouth; here is that which will give language to you, cat: open your mouth; this will shake your shaking, I can tell you, and that soundly: you cannot tell who's your friend: open your chaps again.
lines: Come on your ways; open your mouth; here is that
which will give language to you, cat: open your
mouth; this will shake your shaking, I can tell you,
and that soundly: you cannot tell who's your friend:
open your chaps again.

speeches.speech: I should know that voice: it should be--but he is drowned; and these are devils: O defend me!
lines: I should know that voice: it should be--but he is
drowned; and these are devils: O defend me!

speeches.speech: Four legs and two voices: a most delicate monster! His forward voice now is to speak well of his friend; his backward voice is to utter foul speeches and to detract. If all the wine in my bottle will recover him, I will help his ague. Come. Amen! I will pour some in thy other mouth.
lines: Four legs and two voices: a most delicate monster!
His forward voice now is to speak well of his
friend; his backward voice is to utter foul speeches
and to detract. If all the wine in my bottle will
recover him, I will help his ague. Come. Amen! I
will pour some in thy other mouth.

speeches.speech: Stephano!
lines: Stephano!

speeches.speech: Doth thy other mouth call me? Mercy, mercy! This is a devil, and no monster: I will leave him; I have no long spoon.
lines: Doth thy other mouth call me? Mercy, mercy! This is
a devil, and no monster: I will leave him; I have no
long spoon.

speeches.speech: Stephano! If thou beest Stephano, touch me and speak to me: for I am Trinculo--be not afeard--thy good friend Trinculo.
lines: Stephano! If thou beest Stephano, touch me and
speak to me: for I am Trinculo--be not afeard--thy
good friend Trinculo.

speeches.speech: If thou beest Trinculo, come forth: I'll pull thee by the lesser legs: if any be Trinculo's legs, these are they. Thou art very Trinculo indeed! How camest thou to be the siege of this moon-calf? can he vent Trinculos?
lines: If thou beest Trinculo, come forth: I'll pull thee
by the lesser legs: if any be Trinculo's legs,
these are they. Thou art very Trinculo indeed! How
camest thou to be the siege of this moon-calf? can
he vent Trinculos?

speeches.speech: I took him to be killed with a thunder-stroke. But art thou not drowned, Stephano? I hope now thou art not drowned. Is the storm overblown? I hid me under the dead moon-calf's gaberdine for fear of the storm. And art thou living, Stephano? O Stephano, two Neapolitans 'scaped!
lines: I took him to be killed with a thunder-stroke. But
art thou not drowned, Stephano? I hope now thou art
not drowned. Is the storm overblown? I hid me
under the dead moon-calf's gaberdine for fear of
the storm. And art thou living, Stephano? O
Stephano, two Neapolitans 'scaped!

speeches.speech: Prithee, do not turn me about; my stomach is not constant.
lines: Prithee, do not turn me about; my stomach is not constant.

speeches.speech: [Aside] These be fine things, an if they be not sprites. That's a brave god and bears celestial liquor. I will kneel to him.
lines: [Aside] These be fine things, an if they be
not sprites.
That's a brave god and bears celestial liquor.
I will kneel to him.

speeches.speech: How didst thou 'scape? How camest thou hither? swear by this bottle how thou camest hither. I escaped upon a butt of sack which the sailors heaved o'erboard, by this bottle; which I made of the bark of a tree with mine own hands since I was cast ashore.
lines: How didst thou 'scape? How camest thou hither?
swear by this bottle how thou camest hither. I
escaped upon a butt of sack which the sailors
heaved o'erboard, by this bottle; which I made of
the bark of a tree with mine own hands since I was
cast ashore.

speeches.speech: I'll swear upon that bottle to be thy true subject; for the liquor is not earthly.
lines: I'll swear upon that bottle to be thy true subject;
for the liquor is not earthly.

speeches.speech: Here; swear then how thou escapedst.
lines: Here; swear then how thou escapedst.

speeches.speech: Swum ashore. man, like a duck: I can swim like a duck, I'll be sworn.
lines: Swum ashore. man, like a duck: I can swim like a
duck, I'll be sworn.

speeches.speech: Here, kiss the book. Though thou canst swim like a duck, thou art made like a goose.
lines: Here, kiss the book. Though thou canst swim like a
duck, thou art made like a goose.

speeches.speech: O Stephano. hast any more of this?
lines: O Stephano. hast any more of this?

speeches.speech: The whole butt, man: my cellar is in a rock by the sea-side where my wine is hid. How now, moon-calf! how does thine ague?
lines: The whole butt, man: my cellar is in a rock by the
sea-side where my wine is hid. How now, moon-calf!
how does thine ague?

speeches.speech: Hast thou not dropp'd from heaven?
lines: Hast thou not dropp'd from heaven?

speeches.speech: Out o' the moon, I do assure thee: I was the man i' the moon when time was.
lines: Out o' the moon, I do assure thee: I was the man i'
the moon when time was.

speeches.speech: I have seen thee in her and I do adore thee: My mistress show'd me thee and thy dog and thy bush.
lines: I have seen thee in her and I do adore thee:
My mistress show'd me thee and thy dog and thy bush.

speeches.speech: Come, swear to that; kiss the book: I will furnish it anon with new contents swear.
lines: Come, swear to that; kiss the book: I will furnish
it anon with new contents swear.

speeches.speech: By this good light, this is a very shallow monster! I afeard of him! A very weak monster! The man i' the moon! A most poor credulous monster! Well drawn, monster, in good sooth!
lines: By this good light, this is a very shallow monster!
I afeard of him! A very weak monster! The man i'
the moon! A most poor credulous monster! Well
drawn, monster, in good sooth!

speeches.speech: I'll show thee every fertile inch o' th' island; And I will kiss thy foot: I prithee, be my god.
lines: I'll show thee every fertile inch o' th' island;
And I will kiss thy foot: I prithee, be my god.

speeches.speech: By this light, a most perfidious and drunken monster! when 's god's asleep, he'll rob his bottle.
lines: By this light, a most perfidious and drunken
monster! when 's god's asleep, he'll rob his bottle.

speeches.speech: I'll kiss thy foot; I'll swear myself thy subject.
lines: I'll kiss thy foot; I'll swear myself thy subject.

speeches.speech: Come on then; down, and swear.
lines: Come on then; down, and swear.

speeches.speech: I shall laugh myself to death at this puppy-headed monster. A most scurvy monster! I could find in my heart to beat him,--
lines: I shall laugh myself to death at this puppy-headed
monster. A most scurvy monster! I could find in my
heart to beat him,--

speeches.speech: Come, kiss.
lines: Come, kiss.

speeches.speech: But that the poor monster's in drink: an abominable monster!
lines: But that the poor monster's in drink: an abominable monster!

speeches.speech: I'll show thee the best springs; I'll pluck thee berries; I'll fish for thee and get thee wood enough. A plague upon the tyrant that I serve! I'll bear him no more sticks, but follow thee, Thou wondrous man.
lines: I'll show thee the best springs; I'll pluck thee berries;
I'll fish for thee and get thee wood enough.
A plague upon the tyrant that I serve!
I'll bear him no more sticks, but follow thee,
Thou wondrous man.

speeches.speech: A most ridiculous monster, to make a wonder of a Poor drunkard!
lines: A most ridiculous monster, to make a wonder of a
Poor drunkard!

speeches.speech: I prithee, let me bring thee where crabs grow; And I with my long nails will dig thee pignuts; Show thee a jay's nest and instruct thee how To snare the nimble marmoset; I'll bring thee To clustering filberts and sometimes I'll get thee Young scamels from the rock. Wilt thou go with me?
lines: I prithee, let me bring thee where crabs grow;
And I with my long nails will dig thee pignuts;
Show thee a jay's nest and instruct thee how
To snare the nimble marmoset; I'll bring thee
To clustering filberts and sometimes I'll get thee
Young scamels from the rock. Wilt thou go with me?

speeches.speech: I prithee now, lead the way without any more talking. Trinculo, the king and all our company else being drowned, we will inherit here: here; bear my bottle: fellow Trinculo, we'll fill him by and by again.
lines: I prithee now, lead the way without any more
talking. Trinculo, the king and all our company
else being drowned, we will inherit here: here;
bear my bottle: fellow Trinculo, we'll fill him by
and by again.

speeches.speech: [Sings drunkenly] Farewell master; farewell, farewell!
lines: [Sings drunkenly]
Farewell master; farewell, farewell!

speeches.speech: A howling monster: a drunken monster!
lines: A howling monster: a drunken monster!

speeches.speech: No more dams I'll make for fish Nor fetch in firing At requiring; Nor scrape trencher, nor wash dish 'Ban, 'Ban, Cacaliban Has a new master: get a new man. Freedom, hey-day! hey-day, freedom! freedom, hey-day, freedom!
lines: No more dams I'll make for fish
Nor fetch in firing
At requiring;
Nor scrape trencher, nor wash dish
'Ban, 'Ban, Cacaliban
Has a new master: get a new man.
Freedom, hey-day! hey-day, freedom! freedom,
hey-day, freedom!

speeches.speech: O brave monster! Lead the way.
lines: O brave monster! Lead the way.

speeches.speech: There be some sports are painful, and their labour Delight in them sets off: some kinds of baseness Are nobly undergone and most poor matters Point to rich ends. This my mean task Would be as heavy to me as odious, but The mistress which I serve quickens what's dead And makes my labours pleasures: O, she is Ten times more gentle than her father's crabbed, And he's composed of harshness. I must remove Some thousands of these logs and pile them up, Upon a sore injunction: my sweet mistress Weeps when she sees me work, and says, such baseness Had never like executor. I forget: But these sweet thoughts do even refresh my labours, Most busy lest, when I do it.
lines: There be some sports are painful, and their labour
Delight in them sets off: some kinds of baseness
Are nobly undergone and most poor matters
Point to rich ends. This my mean task
Would be as heavy to me as odious, but
The mistress which I serve quickens what's dead
And makes my labours pleasures: O, she is
Ten times more gentle than her father's crabbed,
And he's composed of harshness. I must remove
Some thousands of these logs and pile them up,
Upon a sore injunction: my sweet mistress
Weeps when she sees me work, and says, such baseness
Had never like executor. I forget:
But these sweet thoughts do even refresh my labours,
Most busy lest, when I do it.

speeches.speech: Alas, now, pray you, Work not so hard: I would the lightning had Burnt up those logs that you are enjoin'd to pile! Pray, set it down and rest you: when this burns, 'Twill weep for having wearied you. My father Is hard at study; pray now, rest yourself; He's safe for these three hours.
lines: Alas, now, pray you,
Work not so hard: I would the lightning had
Burnt up those logs that you are enjoin'd to pile!
Pray, set it down and rest you: when this burns,
'Twill weep for having wearied you. My father
Is hard at study; pray now, rest yourself;
He's safe for these three hours.

speeches.speech: O most dear mistress, The sun will set before I shall discharge What I must strive to do.
lines: O most dear mistress,
The sun will set before I shall discharge
What I must strive to do.

speeches.speech: If you'll sit down, I'll bear your logs the while: pray, give me that; I'll carry it to the pile.
lines: If you'll sit down,
I'll bear your logs the while: pray, give me that;
I'll carry it to the pile.

speeches.speech: No, precious creature; I had rather crack my sinews, break my back, Than you should such dishonour undergo, While I sit lazy by.
lines: No, precious creature;
I had rather crack my sinews, break my back,
Than you should such dishonour undergo,
While I sit lazy by.

speeches.speech: It would become me As well as it does you: and I should do it With much more ease; for my good will is to it, And yours it is against.
lines: It would become me
As well as it does you: and I should do it
With much more ease; for my good will is to it,
And yours it is against.

speeches.speech: Poor worm, thou art infected! This visitation shows it.
lines: Poor worm, thou art infected!
This visitation shows it.

speeches.speech: You look wearily.
lines: You look wearily.

speeches.speech: No, noble mistress;'tis fresh morning with me When you are by at night. I do beseech you-- Chiefly that I might set it in my prayers-- What is your name?
lines: No, noble mistress;'tis fresh morning with me
When you are by at night. I do beseech you--
Chiefly that I might set it in my prayers--
What is your name?

speeches.speech: Miranda.--O my father, I have broke your hest to say so!
lines: Miranda.--O my father,
I have broke your hest to say so!

speeches.speech: Admired Miranda! Indeed the top of admiration! worth What's dearest to the world! Full many a lady I have eyed with best regard and many a time The harmony of their tongues hath into bondage Brought my too diligent ear: for several virtues Have I liked several women; never any With so fun soul, but some defect in her Did quarrel with the noblest grace she owed And put it to the foil: but you, O you, So perfect and so peerless, are created Of every creature's best!
lines: Admired Miranda!
Indeed the top of admiration! worth
What's dearest to the world! Full many a lady
I have eyed with best regard and many a time
The harmony of their tongues hath into bondage
Brought my too diligent ear: for several virtues
Have I liked several women; never any
With so fun soul, but some defect in her
Did quarrel with the noblest grace she owed
And put it to the foil: but you, O you,
So perfect and so peerless, are created
Of every creature's best!

speeches.speech: I do not know One of my sex; no woman's face remember, Save, from my glass, mine own; nor have I seen More that I may call men than you, good friend, And my dear father: how features are abroad, I am skilless of; but, by my modesty, The jewel in my dower, I would not wish Any companion in the world but you, Nor can imagination form a shape, Besides yourself, to like of. But I prattle Something too wildly and my father's precepts I therein do forget.
lines: I do not know
One of my sex; no woman's face remember,
Save, from my glass, mine own; nor have I seen
More that I may call men than you, good friend,
And my dear father: how features are abroad,
I am skilless of; but, by my modesty,
The jewel in my dower, I would not wish
Any companion in the world but you,
Nor can imagination form a shape,
Besides yourself, to like of. But I prattle
Something too wildly and my father's precepts
I therein do forget.

speeches.speech: I am in my condition A prince, Miranda; I do think, a king; I would, not so!--and would no more endure This wooden slavery than to suffer The flesh-fly blow my mouth. Hear my soul speak: The very instant that I saw you, did My heart fly to your service; there resides, To make me slave to it; and for your sake Am I this patient log--man.
lines: I am in my condition
A prince, Miranda; I do think, a king;
I would, not so!--and would no more endure
This wooden slavery than to suffer
The flesh-fly blow my mouth. Hear my soul speak:
The very instant that I saw you, did
My heart fly to your service; there resides,
To make me slave to it; and for your sake
Am I this patient log--man.

speeches.speech: Do you love me?
lines: Do you love me?

speeches.speech: O heaven, O earth, bear witness to this sound And crown what I profess with kind event If I speak true! if hollowly, invert What best is boded me to mischief! I Beyond all limit of what else i' the world Do love, prize, honour you.
lines: O heaven, O earth, bear witness to this sound
And crown what I profess with kind event
If I speak true! if hollowly, invert
What best is boded me to mischief! I
Beyond all limit of what else i' the world
Do love, prize, honour you.

speeches.speech: I am a fool To weep at what I am glad of.
lines: I am a fool
To weep at what I am glad of.

speeches.speech: Fair encounter Of two most rare affections! Heavens rain grace On that which breeds between 'em!
lines: Fair encounter
Of two most rare affections! Heavens rain grace
On that which breeds between 'em!

speeches.speech: Wherefore weep you?
lines: Wherefore weep you?

speeches.speech: At mine unworthiness that dare not offer What I desire to give, and much less take What I shall die to want. But this is trifling; And all the more it seeks to hide itself, The bigger bulk it shows. Hence, bashful cunning! And prompt me, plain and holy innocence! I am your wife, it you will marry me; If not, I'll die your maid: to be your fellow You may deny me; but I'll be your servant, Whether you will or no.
lines: At mine unworthiness that dare not offer
What I desire to give, and much less take
What I shall die to want. But this is trifling;
And all the more it seeks to hide itself,
The bigger bulk it shows. Hence, bashful cunning!
And prompt me, plain and holy innocence!
I am your wife, it you will marry me;
If not, I'll die your maid: to be your fellow
You may deny me; but I'll be your servant,
Whether you will or no.

speeches.speech: My mistress, dearest; And I thus humble ever.
lines: My mistress, dearest;
And I thus humble ever.

speeches.speech: My husband, then?
lines: My husband, then?

speeches.speech: Ay, with a heart as willing As bondage e'er of freedom: here's my hand.
lines: Ay, with a heart as willing
As bondage e'er of freedom: here's my hand.

speeches.speech: And mine, with my heart in't; and now farewell Till half an hour hence.
lines: And mine, with my heart in't; and now farewell
Till half an hour hence.

speeches.speech: A thousand thousand!
lines: A thousand thousand!

speeches.speech: So glad of this as they I cannot be, Who are surprised withal; but my rejoicing At nothing can be more. I'll to my book, For yet ere supper-time must I perform Much business appertaining.
lines: So glad of this as they I cannot be,
Who are surprised withal; but my rejoicing
At nothing can be more. I'll to my book,
For yet ere supper-time must I perform
Much business appertaining.

speeches.speech: Tell not me; when the butt is out, we will drink water; not a drop before: therefore bear up, and board 'em. Servant-monster, drink to me.
lines: Tell not me; when the butt is out, we will drink
water; not a drop before: therefore bear up, and
board 'em. Servant-monster, drink to me.

speeches.speech: Servant-monster! the folly of this island! They say there's but five upon this isle: we are three of them; if th' other two be brained like us, the state totters.
lines: Servant-monster! the folly of this island! They
say there's but five upon this isle: we are three
of them; if th' other two be brained like us, the
state totters.

speeches.speech: Drink, servant-monster, when I bid thee: thy eyes are almost set in thy head.
lines: Drink, servant-monster, when I bid thee: thy eyes
are almost set in thy head.

speeches.speech: Where should they be set else? he were a brave monster indeed, if they were set in his tail.
lines: Where should they be set else? he were a brave
monster indeed, if they were set in his tail.

speeches.speech: My man-monster hath drown'd his tongue in sack: for my part, the sea cannot drown me; I swam, ere I could recover the shore, five and thirty leagues off and on. By this light, thou shalt be my lieutenant, monster, or my standard.
lines: My man-monster hath drown'd his tongue in sack:
for my part, the sea cannot drown me; I swam, ere I
could recover the shore, five and thirty leagues off
and on. By this light, thou shalt be my lieutenant,
monster, or my standard.

speeches.speech: Your lieutenant, if you list; he's no standard.
lines: Your lieutenant, if you list; he's no standard.

speeches.speech: We'll not run, Monsieur Monster.
lines: We'll not run, Monsieur Monster.

speeches.speech: Nor go neither; but you'll lie like dogs and yet say nothing neither.
lines: Nor go neither; but you'll lie like dogs and yet say
nothing neither.

speeches.speech: Moon-calf, speak once in thy life, if thou beest a good moon-calf.
lines: Moon-calf, speak once in thy life, if thou beest a
good moon-calf.

speeches.speech: How does thy honour? Let me lick thy shoe. I'll not serve him; he's not valiant.
lines: How does thy honour? Let me lick thy shoe.
I'll not serve him; he's not valiant.

speeches.speech: Thou liest, most ignorant monster: I am in case to justle a constable. Why, thou deboshed fish thou, was there ever man a coward that hath drunk so much sack as I to-day? Wilt thou tell a monstrous lie, being but half a fish and half a monster?
lines: Thou liest, most ignorant monster: I am in case to
justle a constable. Why, thou deboshed fish thou,
was there ever man a coward that hath drunk so much
sack as I to-day? Wilt thou tell a monstrous lie,
being but half a fish and half a monster?

speeches.speech: Lo, how he mocks me! wilt thou let him, my lord?
lines: Lo, how he mocks me! wilt thou let him, my lord?

speeches.speech: 'Lord' quoth he! That a monster should be such a natural!
lines: 'Lord' quoth he! That a monster should be such a natural!

speeches.speech: Lo, lo, again! bite him to death, I prithee.
lines: Lo, lo, again! bite him to death, I prithee.

speeches.speech: Trinculo, keep a good tongue in your head: if you prove a mutineer,--the next tree! The poor monster's my subject and he shall not suffer indignity.
lines: Trinculo, keep a good tongue in your head: if you
prove a mutineer,--the next tree! The poor monster's
my subject and he shall not suffer indignity.

speeches.speech: I thank my noble lord. Wilt thou be pleased to hearken once again to the suit I made to thee?
lines: I thank my noble lord. Wilt thou be pleased to
hearken once again to the suit I made to thee?

speeches.speech: Marry, will I. kneel and repeat it; I will stand, and so shall Trinculo.
lines: Marry, will I. kneel and repeat it; I will stand,
and so shall Trinculo.

speeches.speech: As I told thee before, I am subject to a tyrant, a sorcerer, that by his cunning hath cheated me of the island.
lines: As I told thee before, I am subject to a tyrant, a
sorcerer, that by his cunning hath cheated me of the island.

speeches.speech: Thou liest.
lines: Thou liest.

speeches.speech: Thou liest, thou jesting monkey, thou: I would my valiant master would destroy thee! I do not lie.
lines: Thou liest, thou jesting monkey, thou: I would my
valiant master would destroy thee! I do not lie.

speeches.speech: Trinculo, if you trouble him any more in's tale, by this hand, I will supplant some of your teeth.
lines: Trinculo, if you trouble him any more in's tale, by
this hand, I will supplant some of your teeth.

speeches.speech: Why, I said nothing.
lines: Why, I said nothing.

speeches.speech: Mum, then, and no more. Proceed.
lines: Mum, then, and no more. Proceed.

speeches.speech: I say, by sorcery he got this isle; From me he got it. if thy greatness will Revenge it on him,--for I know thou darest, But this thing dare not,--
lines: I say, by sorcery he got this isle;
From me he got it. if thy greatness will
Revenge it on him,--for I know thou darest,
But this thing dare not,--

speeches.speech: That's most certain.
lines: That's most certain.

speeches.speech: Thou shalt be lord of it and I'll serve thee.
lines: Thou shalt be lord of it and I'll serve thee.

speeches.speech: How now shall this be compassed? Canst thou bring me to the party?
lines: How now shall this be compassed?
Canst thou bring me to the party?

speeches.speech: Yea, yea, my lord: I'll yield him thee asleep, Where thou mayst knock a nail into his bead.
lines: Yea, yea, my lord: I'll yield him thee asleep,
Where thou mayst knock a nail into his bead.

speeches.speech: Thou liest; thou canst not.
lines: Thou liest; thou canst not.

speeches.speech: What a pied ninny's this! Thou scurvy patch! I do beseech thy greatness, give him blows And take his bottle from him: when that's gone He shall drink nought but brine; for I'll not show him Where the quick freshes are.
lines: What a pied ninny's this! Thou scurvy patch!
I do beseech thy greatness, give him blows
And take his bottle from him: when that's gone
He shall drink nought but brine; for I'll not show him
Where the quick freshes are.

speeches.speech: Trinculo, run into no further danger: interrupt the monster one word further, and, by this hand, I'll turn my mercy out o' doors and make a stock-fish of thee.
lines: Trinculo, run into no further danger:
interrupt the monster one word further, and,
by this hand, I'll turn my mercy out o' doors
and make a stock-fish of thee.

speeches.speech: Why, what did I? I did nothing. I'll go farther off.
lines: Why, what did I? I did nothing. I'll go farther
off.

speeches.speech: Didst thou not say he lied?
lines: Didst thou not say he lied?

speeches.speech: Thou liest.
lines: Thou liest.

speeches.speech: Do I so? take thou that. [Beats TRINCULO] As you like this, give me the lie another time.
lines: Do I so? take thou that.
[Beats TRINCULO]
As you like this, give me the lie another time.

speeches.speech: I did not give the lie. Out o' your wits and bearing too? A pox o' your bottle! this can sack and drinking do. A murrain on your monster, and the devil take your fingers!
lines: I did not give the lie. Out o' your
wits and bearing too? A pox o' your bottle!
this can sack and drinking do. A murrain on
your monster, and the devil take your fingers!

speeches.speech: Ha, ha, ha!
lines: Ha, ha, ha!

speeches.speech: Now, forward with your tale. Prithee, stand farther off.
lines: Now, forward with your tale. Prithee, stand farther
off.

speeches.speech: Beat him enough: after a little time I'll beat him too.
lines: Beat him enough: after a little time
I'll beat him too.

speeches.speech: Stand farther. Come, proceed.
lines: Stand farther. Come, proceed.

speeches.speech: Why, as I told thee, 'tis a custom with him, I' th' afternoon to sleep: there thou mayst brain him, Having first seized his books, or with a log Batter his skull, or paunch him with a stake, Or cut his wezand with thy knife. Remember First to possess his books; for without them He's but a sot, as I am, nor hath not One spirit to command: they all do hate him As rootedly as I. Burn but his books. He has brave utensils,--for so he calls them-- Which when he has a house, he'll deck withal And that most deeply to consider is The beauty of his daughter; he himself Calls her a nonpareil: I never saw a woman, But only Sycorax my dam and she; But she as far surpasseth Sycorax As great'st does least.
lines: Why, as I told thee, 'tis a custom with him,
I' th' afternoon to sleep: there thou mayst brain him,
Having first seized his books, or with a log
Batter his skull, or paunch him with a stake,
Or cut his wezand with thy knife. Remember
First to possess his books; for without them
He's but a sot, as I am, nor hath not
One spirit to command: they all do hate him
As rootedly as I. Burn but his books.
He has brave utensils,--for so he calls them--
Which when he has a house, he'll deck withal
And that most deeply to consider is
The beauty of his daughter; he himself
Calls her a nonpareil: I never saw a woman,
But only Sycorax my dam and she;
But she as far surpasseth Sycorax
As great'st does least.

speeches.speech: Is it so brave a lass?
lines: Is it so brave a lass?

speeches.speech: Ay, lord; she will become thy bed, I warrant. And bring thee forth brave brood.
lines: Ay, lord; she will become thy bed, I warrant.
And bring thee forth brave brood.

speeches.speech: Monster, I will kill this man: his daughter and I will be king and queen--save our graces!--and Trinculo and thyself shall be viceroys. Dost thou like the plot, Trinculo?
lines: Monster, I will kill this man: his daughter and I
will be king and queen--save our graces!--and
Trinculo and thyself shall be viceroys. Dost thou
like the plot, Trinculo?

speeches.speech: Excellent.
lines: Excellent.

speeches.speech: Give me thy hand: I am sorry I beat thee; but, while thou livest, keep a good tongue in thy head.
lines: Give me thy hand: I am sorry I beat thee; but,
while thou livest, keep a good tongue in thy head.

speeches.speech: Within this half hour will he be asleep: Wilt thou destroy him then?
lines: Within this half hour will he be asleep:
Wilt thou destroy him then?

speeches.speech: Ay, on mine honour.
lines: Ay, on mine honour.

speeches.speech: This will I tell my master.
lines: This will I tell my master.

speeches.speech: Thou makest me merry; I am full of pleasure: Let us be jocund: will you troll the catch You taught me but while-ere?
lines: Thou makest me merry; I am full of pleasure:
Let us be jocund: will you troll the catch
You taught me but while-ere?

speeches.speech: At thy request, monster, I will do reason, any reason. Come on, Trinculo, let us sing. [Sings] Flout 'em and scout 'em And scout 'em and flout 'em Thought is free.
lines: At thy request, monster, I will do reason, any
reason. Come on, Trinculo, let us sing.
[Sings]
Flout 'em and scout 'em
And scout 'em and flout 'em
Thought is free.

speeches.speech: That's not the tune.
lines: That's not the tune.

speeches.speech: What is this same?
lines: What is this same?

speeches.speech: This is the tune of our catch, played by the picture of Nobody.
lines: This is the tune of our catch, played by the picture
of Nobody.

speeches.speech: If thou beest a man, show thyself in thy likeness: if thou beest a devil, take't as thou list.
lines: If thou beest a man, show thyself in thy likeness:
if thou beest a devil, take't as thou list.

speeches.speech: O, forgive me my sins!
lines: O, forgive me my sins!

speeches.speech: He that dies pays all debts: I defy thee. Mercy upon us!
lines: He that dies pays all debts: I defy thee. Mercy upon us!

speeches.speech: Art thou afeard?
lines: Art thou afeard?

speeches.speech: No, monster, not I.
lines: No, monster, not I.

speeches.speech: Be not afeard; the isle is full of noises, Sounds and sweet airs, that give delight and hurt not. Sometimes a thousand twangling instruments Will hum about mine ears, and sometime voices That, if I then had waked after long sleep, Will make me sleep again: and then, in dreaming, The clouds methought would open and show riches Ready to drop upon me that, when I waked, I cried to dream again.
lines: Be not afeard; the isle is full of noises,
Sounds and sweet airs, that give delight and hurt not.
Sometimes a thousand twangling instruments
Will hum about mine ears, and sometime voices
That, if I then had waked after long sleep,
Will make me sleep again: and then, in dreaming,
The clouds methought would open and show riches
Ready to drop upon me that, when I waked,
I cried to dream again.

speeches.speech: This will prove a brave kingdom to me, where I shall have my music for nothing.
lines: This will prove a brave kingdom to me, where I shall
have my music for nothing.

speeches.speech: When Prospero is destroyed.
lines: When Prospero is destroyed.

speeches.speech: That shall be by and by: I remember the story.
lines: That shall be by and by: I remember the story.

speeches.speech: The sound is going away; let's follow it, and after do our work.
lines: The sound is going away; let's follow it, and
after do our work.

speeches.speech: Lead, monster; we'll follow. I would I could see this tabourer; he lays it on.
lines: Lead, monster; we'll follow. I would I could see
this tabourer; he lays it on.

speeches.speech: Wilt come? I'll follow, Stephano.
lines: Wilt come? I'll follow, Stephano.

speeches.speech: By'r lakin, I can go no further, sir; My old bones ache: here's a maze trod indeed Through forth-rights and meanders! By your patience, I needs must rest me.
lines: By'r lakin, I can go no further, sir;
My old bones ache: here's a maze trod indeed
Through forth-rights and meanders! By your patience,
I needs must rest me.

speeches.speech: Old lord, I cannot blame thee, Who am myself attach'd with weariness, To the dulling of my spirits: sit down, and rest. Even here I will put off my hope and keep it No longer for my flatterer: he is drown'd Whom thus we stray to find, and the sea mocks Our frustrate search on land. Well, let him go.
lines: Old lord, I cannot blame thee,
Who am myself attach'd with weariness,
To the dulling of my spirits: sit down, and rest.
Even here I will put off my hope and keep it
No longer for my flatterer: he is drown'd
Whom thus we stray to find, and the sea mocks
Our frustrate search on land. Well, let him go.

speeches.speech: [Aside to SEBASTIAN] I am right glad that he's so out of hope. Do not, for one repulse, forego the purpose That you resolved to effect.
lines: [Aside to SEBASTIAN] I am right glad that he's so
out of hope.
Do not, for one repulse, forego the purpose
That you resolved to effect.

speeches.speech: [Aside to ANTONIO] The next advantage Will we take throughly.
lines: [Aside to ANTONIO] The next advantage
Will we take throughly.

speeches.speech: [Aside to SEBASTIAN] Let it be to-night; For, now they are oppress'd with travel, they Will not, nor cannot, use such vigilance As when they are fresh.
lines: [Aside to SEBASTIAN] Let it be to-night;
For, now they are oppress'd with travel, they
Will not, nor cannot, use such vigilance
As when they are fresh.

speeches.speech: [Aside to ANTONIO] I say, to-night: no more.
lines: [Aside to ANTONIO] I say, to-night: no more.

speeches.speech: What harmony is this? My good friends, hark!
lines: What harmony is this? My good friends, hark!

speeches.speech: Marvellous sweet music! [Enter PROSPERO above, invisible. Enter several] strange Shapes, bringing in a banquet; they dance about it with gentle actions of salutation; and, inviting the King, &c. to eat, they depart]
lines: Marvellous sweet music!
[Enter PROSPERO above, invisible. Enter several]
strange Shapes, bringing in a banquet;
they dance about it with gentle actions of
salutation; and, inviting the King, &c. to
eat, they depart]

speeches.speech: Give us kind keepers, heavens! What were these?
lines: Give us kind keepers, heavens! What were these?

speeches.speech: A living drollery. Now I will believe That there are unicorns, that in Arabia There is one tree, the phoenix' throne, one phoenix At this hour reigning there.
lines: A living drollery. Now I will believe
That there are unicorns, that in Arabia
There is one tree, the phoenix' throne, one phoenix
At this hour reigning there.

speeches.speech: I'll believe both; And what does else want credit, come to me, And I'll be sworn 'tis true: travellers ne'er did lie, Though fools at home condemn 'em.
lines: I'll believe both;
And what does else want credit, come to me,
And I'll be sworn 'tis true: travellers ne'er did
lie,
Though fools at home condemn 'em.

speeches.speech: If in Naples I should report this now, would they believe me? If I should say, I saw such islanders-- For, certes, these are people of the island-- Who, though they are of monstrous shape, yet, note, Their manners are more gentle-kind than of Our human generation you shall find Many, nay, almost any.
lines: If in Naples
I should report this now, would they believe me?
If I should say, I saw such islanders--
For, certes, these are people of the island--
Who, though they are of monstrous shape, yet, note,
Their manners are more gentle-kind than of
Our human generation you shall find
Many, nay, almost any.

speeches.speech: [Aside] Honest lord, Thou hast said well; for some of you there present Are worse than devils.
lines: [Aside] Honest lord,
Thou hast said well; for some of you there present
Are worse than devils.

speeches.speech: I cannot too much muse Such shapes, such gesture and such sound, expressing, Although they want the use of tongue, a kind Of excellent dumb discourse.
lines: I cannot too much muse
Such shapes, such gesture and such sound, expressing,
Although they want the use of tongue, a kind
Of excellent dumb discourse.

speeches.speech: [Aside]. Praise in departing.
lines: [Aside]. Praise in departing.

speeches.speech: They vanish'd strangely.
lines: They vanish'd strangely.

speeches.speech: No matter, since They have left their viands behind; for we have stomachs. Will't please you taste of what is here?
lines: No matter, since
They have left their viands behind; for we have stomachs.
Will't please you taste of what is here?

speeches.speech: Not I.
lines: Not I.

speeches.speech: Faith, sir, you need not fear. When we were boys, Who would believe that there were mountaineers Dew-lapp'd like bulls, whose throats had hanging at 'em Wallets of flesh? or that there were such men Whose heads stood in their breasts? which now we find Each putter-out of five for one will bring us Good warrant of.
lines: Faith, sir, you need not fear. When we were boys,
Who would believe that there were mountaineers
Dew-lapp'd like bulls, whose throats had hanging at 'em
Wallets of flesh? or that there were such men
Whose heads stood in their breasts? which now we find
Each putter-out of five for one will bring us
Good warrant of.

speeches.speech: I will stand to and feed, Although my last: no matter, since I feel The best is past. Brother, my lord the duke, Stand to and do as we. [Thunder and lightning. Enter ARIEL, like a] harpy; claps his wings upon the table; and, with a quaint device, the banquet vanishes]
lines: I will stand to and feed,
Although my last: no matter, since I feel
The best is past. Brother, my lord the duke,
Stand to and do as we.
[Thunder and lightning. Enter ARIEL, like a]
harpy; claps his wings upon the table; and,
with a quaint device, the banquet vanishes]

speeches.speech: You are three men of sin, whom Destiny, That hath to instrument this lower world And what is in't, the never-surfeited sea Hath caused to belch up you; and on this island Where man doth not inhabit; you 'mongst men Being most unfit to live. I have made you mad; And even with such-like valour men hang and drown Their proper selves. [ALONSO, SEBASTIAN &c. draw their swords] You fools! I and my fellows Are ministers of Fate: the elements, Of whom your swords are temper'd, may as well Wound the loud winds, or with bemock'd-at stabs Kill the still-closing waters, as diminish One dowle that's in my plume: my fellow-ministers Are like invulnerable. If you could hurt, Your swords are now too massy for your strengths And will not be uplifted. But remember-- For that's my business to you--that you three From Milan did supplant good Prospero; Exposed unto the sea, which hath requit it, Him and his innocent child: for which foul deed The powers, delaying, not forgetting, have Incensed the seas and shores, yea, all the creatures, Against your peace. Thee of thy son, Alonso, They have bereft; and do pronounce by me: Lingering perdition, worse than any death Can be at once, shall step by step attend You and your ways; whose wraths to guard you from-- Which here, in this most desolate isle, else falls Upon your heads--is nothing but heart-sorrow And a clear life ensuing. [He vanishes in thunder; then, to soft music] enter the Shapes again, and dance, with mocks and mows, and carrying out the table]
lines: You are three men of sin, whom Destiny,
That hath to instrument this lower world
And what is in't, the never-surfeited sea
Hath caused to belch up you; and on this island
Where man doth not inhabit; you 'mongst men
Being most unfit to live. I have made you mad;
And even with such-like valour men hang and drown
Their proper selves.
[ALONSO, SEBASTIAN &c. draw their swords]
You fools! I and my fellows
Are ministers of Fate: the elements,
Of whom your swords are temper'd, may as well
Wound the loud winds, or with bemock'd-at stabs
Kill the still-closing waters, as diminish
One dowle that's in my plume: my fellow-ministers
Are like invulnerable. If you could hurt,
Your swords are now too massy for your strengths
And will not be uplifted. But remember--
For that's my business to you--that you three
From Milan did supplant good Prospero;
Exposed unto the sea, which hath requit it,
Him and his innocent child: for which foul deed
The powers, delaying, not forgetting, have
Incensed the seas and shores, yea, all the creatures,
Against your peace. Thee of thy son, Alonso,
They have bereft; and do pronounce by me:
Lingering perdition, worse than any death
Can be at once, shall step by step attend
You and your ways; whose wraths to guard you from--
Which here, in this most desolate isle, else falls
Upon your heads--is nothing but heart-sorrow
And a clear life ensuing.
[He vanishes in thunder; then, to soft music]
enter the Shapes again, and dance, with
mocks and mows, and carrying out the table]

speeches.speech: Bravely the figure of this harpy hast thou Perform'd, my Ariel; a grace it had, devouring: Of my instruction hast thou nothing bated In what thou hadst to say: so, with good life And observation strange, my meaner ministers Their several kinds have done. My high charms work And these mine enemies are all knit up In their distractions; they now are in my power; And in these fits I leave them, while I visit Young Ferdinand, whom they suppose is drown'd, And his and mine loved darling.
lines: Bravely the figure of this harpy hast thou
Perform'd, my Ariel; a grace it had, devouring:
Of my instruction hast thou nothing bated
In what thou hadst to say: so, with good life
And observation strange, my meaner ministers
Their several kinds have done. My high charms work
And these mine enemies are all knit up
In their distractions; they now are in my power;
And in these fits I leave them, while I visit
Young Ferdinand, whom they suppose is drown'd,
And his and mine loved darling.

speeches.speech: I' the name of something holy, sir, why stand you In this strange stare?
lines: I' the name of something holy, sir, why stand you
In this strange stare?

speeches.speech: O, it is monstrous, monstrous: Methought the billows spoke and told me of it; The winds did sing it to me, and the thunder, That deep and dreadful organ-pipe, pronounced The name of Prosper: it did bass my trespass. Therefore my son i' the ooze is bedded, and I'll seek him deeper than e'er plummet sounded And with him there lie mudded.
lines: O, it is monstrous, monstrous:
Methought the billows spoke and told me of it;
The winds did sing it to me, and the thunder,
That deep and dreadful organ-pipe, pronounced
The name of Prosper: it did bass my trespass.
Therefore my son i' the ooze is bedded, and
I'll seek him deeper than e'er plummet sounded
And with him there lie mudded.

speeches.speech: But one fiend at a time, I'll fight their legions o'er.
lines: But one fiend at a time,
I'll fight their legions o'er.

speeches.speech: I'll be thy second.
lines: I'll be thy second.

speeches.speech: All three of them are desperate: their great guilt, Like poison given to work a great time after, Now 'gins to bite the spirits. I do beseech you That are of suppler joints, follow them swiftly And hinder them from what this ecstasy May now provoke them to.
lines: All three of them are desperate: their great guilt,
Like poison given to work a great time after,
Now 'gins to bite the spirits. I do beseech you
That are of suppler joints, follow them swiftly
And hinder them from what this ecstasy
May now provoke them to.

speeches.speech: Follow, I pray you.
lines: Follow, I pray you.

speeches.speech: If I have too austerely punish'd you, Your compensation makes amends, for I Have given you here a third of mine own life, Or that for which I live; who once again I tender to thy hand: all thy vexations Were but my trials of thy love and thou Hast strangely stood the test here, afore Heaven, I ratify this my rich gift. O Ferdinand, Do not smile at me that I boast her off, For thou shalt find she will outstrip all praise And make it halt behind her.
lines: If I have too austerely punish'd you,
Your compensation makes amends, for I
Have given you here a third of mine own life,
Or that for which I live; who once again
I tender to thy hand: all thy vexations
Were but my trials of thy love and thou
Hast strangely stood the test here, afore Heaven,
I ratify this my rich gift. O Ferdinand,
Do not smile at me that I boast her off,
For thou shalt find she will outstrip all praise
And make it halt behind her.

speeches.speech: I do believe it Against an oracle.
lines: I do believe it
Against an oracle.

speeches.speech: Then, as my gift and thine own acquisition Worthily purchased take my daughter: but If thou dost break her virgin-knot before All sanctimonious ceremonies may With full and holy rite be minister'd, No sweet aspersion shall the heavens let fall To make this contract grow: but barren hate, Sour-eyed disdain and discord shall bestrew The union of your bed with weeds so loathly That you shall hate it both: therefore take heed, As Hymen's lamps shall light you.
lines: Then, as my gift and thine own acquisition
Worthily purchased take my daughter: but
If thou dost break her virgin-knot before
All sanctimonious ceremonies may
With full and holy rite be minister'd,
No sweet aspersion shall the heavens let fall
To make this contract grow: but barren hate,
Sour-eyed disdain and discord shall bestrew
The union of your bed with weeds so loathly
That you shall hate it both: therefore take heed,
As Hymen's lamps shall light you.

speeches.speech: As I hope For quiet days, fair issue and long life, With such love as 'tis now, the murkiest den, The most opportune place, the strong'st suggestion. Our worser genius can, shall never melt Mine honour into lust, to take away The edge of that day's celebration When I shall think: or Phoebus' steeds are founder'd, Or Night kept chain'd below.
lines: As I hope
For quiet days, fair issue and long life,
With such love as 'tis now, the murkiest den,
The most opportune place, the strong'st suggestion.
Our worser genius can, shall never melt
Mine honour into lust, to take away
The edge of that day's celebration
When I shall think: or Phoebus' steeds are founder'd,
Or Night kept chain'd below.

speeches.speech: Fairly spoke. Sit then and talk with her; she is thine own. What, Ariel! my industrious servant, Ariel!
lines: Fairly spoke.
Sit then and talk with her; she is thine own.
What, Ariel! my industrious servant, Ariel!

speeches.speech: What would my potent master? here I am.
lines: What would my potent master? here I am.

speeches.speech: Thou and thy meaner fellows your last service Did worthily perform; and I must use you In such another trick. Go bring the rabble, O'er whom I give thee power, here to this place: Incite them to quick motion; for I must Bestow upon the eyes of this young couple Some vanity of mine art: it is my promise, And they expect it from me.
lines: Thou and thy meaner fellows your last service
Did worthily perform; and I must use you
In such another trick. Go bring the rabble,
O'er whom I give thee power, here to this place:
Incite them to quick motion; for I must
Bestow upon the eyes of this young couple
Some vanity of mine art: it is my promise,
And they expect it from me.

speeches.speech: Presently?
lines: Presently?

speeches.speech: Ay, with a twink.
lines: Ay, with a twink.

speeches.speech: Before you can say 'come' and 'go,' And breathe twice and cry 'so, so,' Each one, tripping on his toe, Will be here with mop and mow. Do you love me, master? no?
lines: Before you can say 'come' and 'go,'
And breathe twice and cry 'so, so,'
Each one, tripping on his toe,
Will be here with mop and mow.
Do you love me, master? no?

speeches.speech: Dearly my delicate Ariel. Do not approach Till thou dost hear me call.
lines: Dearly my delicate Ariel. Do not approach
Till thou dost hear me call.

speeches.speech: Well, I conceive.
lines: Well, I conceive.

speeches.speech: Look thou be true; do not give dalliance Too much the rein: the strongest oaths are straw To the fire i' the blood: be more abstemious, Or else, good night your vow!
lines: Look thou be true; do not give dalliance
Too much the rein: the strongest oaths are straw
To the fire i' the blood: be more abstemious,
Or else, good night your vow!

speeches.speech: I warrant you sir; The white cold virgin snow upon my heart Abates the ardour of my liver.
lines: I warrant you sir;
The white cold virgin snow upon my heart
Abates the ardour of my liver.

speeches.speech: Well. Now come, my Ariel! bring a corollary, Rather than want a spirit: appear and pertly! No tongue! all eyes! be silent.
lines: Well.
Now come, my Ariel! bring a corollary,
Rather than want a spirit: appear and pertly!
No tongue! all eyes! be silent.

speeches.speech: Ceres, most bounteous lady, thy rich leas Of wheat, rye, barley, vetches, oats and pease; Thy turfy mountains, where live nibbling sheep, And flat meads thatch'd with stover, them to keep; Thy banks with pioned and twilled brims, Which spongy April at thy hest betrims, To make cold nymphs chaste crowns; and thy broom -groves, Whose shadow the dismissed bachelor loves, Being lass-lorn: thy pole-clipt vineyard; And thy sea-marge, sterile and rocky-hard, Where thou thyself dost air;--the queen o' the sky, Whose watery arch and messenger am I, Bids thee leave these, and with her sovereign grace, Here on this grass-plot, in this very place, To come and sport: her peacocks fly amain: Approach, rich Ceres, her to entertain.
lines: Ceres, most bounteous lady, thy rich leas
Of wheat, rye, barley, vetches, oats and pease;
Thy turfy mountains, where live nibbling sheep,
And flat meads thatch'd with stover, them to keep;
Thy banks with pioned and twilled brims,
Which spongy April at thy hest betrims,
To make cold nymphs chaste crowns; and thy broom -groves,
Whose shadow the dismissed bachelor loves,
Being lass-lorn: thy pole-clipt vineyard;
And thy sea-marge, sterile and rocky-hard,
Where thou thyself dost air;--the queen o' the sky,
Whose watery arch and messenger am I,
Bids thee leave these, and with her sovereign grace,
Here on this grass-plot, in this very place,
To come and sport: her peacocks fly amain:
Approach, rich Ceres, her to entertain.

speeches.speech: Hail, many-colour'd messenger, that ne'er Dost disobey the wife of Jupiter; Who with thy saffron wings upon my flowers Diffusest honey-drops, refreshing showers, And with each end of thy blue bow dost crown My bosky acres and my unshrubb'd down, Rich scarf to my proud earth; why hath thy queen Summon'd me hither, to this short-grass'd green?
lines: Hail, many-colour'd messenger, that ne'er
Dost disobey the wife of Jupiter;
Who with thy saffron wings upon my flowers
Diffusest honey-drops, refreshing showers,
And with each end of thy blue bow dost crown
My bosky acres and my unshrubb'd down,
Rich scarf to my proud earth; why hath thy queen
Summon'd me hither, to this short-grass'd green?

speeches.speech: A contract of true love to celebrate; And some donation freely to estate On the blest lovers.
lines: A contract of true love to celebrate;
And some donation freely to estate
On the blest lovers.

speeches.speech: Tell me, heavenly bow, If Venus or her son, as thou dost know, Do now attend the queen? Since they did plot The means that dusky Dis my daughter got, Her and her blind boy's scandal'd company I have forsworn.
lines: Tell me, heavenly bow,
If Venus or her son, as thou dost know,
Do now attend the queen? Since they did plot
The means that dusky Dis my daughter got,
Her and her blind boy's scandal'd company
I have forsworn.

speeches.speech: Of her society Be not afraid: I met her deity Cutting the clouds towards Paphos and her son Dove-drawn with her. Here thought they to have done Some wanton charm upon this man and maid, Whose vows are, that no bed-right shall be paid Till Hymen's torch be lighted: but vain; Mars's hot minion is returned again; Her waspish-headed son has broke his arrows, Swears he will shoot no more but play with sparrows And be a boy right out.
lines: Of her society
Be not afraid: I met her deity
Cutting the clouds towards Paphos and her son
Dove-drawn with her. Here thought they to have done
Some wanton charm upon this man and maid,
Whose vows are, that no bed-right shall be paid
Till Hymen's torch be lighted: but vain;
Mars's hot minion is returned again;
Her waspish-headed son has broke his arrows,
Swears he will shoot no more but play with sparrows
And be a boy right out.

speeches.speech: High'st queen of state, Great Juno, comes; I know her by her gait.
lines: High'st queen of state,
Great Juno, comes; I know her by her gait.

speeches.speech: How does my bounteous sister? Go with me To bless this twain, that they may prosperous be And honour'd in their issue.
lines: How does my bounteous sister? Go with me
To bless this twain, that they may prosperous be
And honour'd in their issue.

speeches.speech: Honour, riches, marriage-blessing, Long continuance, and increasing, Hourly joys be still upon you! Juno sings her blessings upon you.
lines: Honour, riches, marriage-blessing,
Long continuance, and increasing,
Hourly joys be still upon you!
Juno sings her blessings upon you.

speeches.speech: Earth's increase, foison plenty, Barns and garners never empty, Vines and clustering bunches growing, Plants with goodly burthen bowing; Spring come to you at the farthest In the very end of harvest! Scarcity and want shall shun you; Ceres' blessing so is on you.
lines: Earth's increase, foison plenty,
Barns and garners never empty,
Vines and clustering bunches growing,
Plants with goodly burthen bowing;
Spring come to you at the farthest
In the very end of harvest!
Scarcity and want shall shun you;
Ceres' blessing so is on you.

speeches.speech: This is a most majestic vision, and Harmoniously charmingly. May I be bold To think these spirits?
lines: This is a most majestic vision, and
Harmoniously charmingly. May I be bold
To think these spirits?

speeches.speech: Spirits, which by mine art I have from their confines call'd to enact My present fancies.
lines: Spirits, which by mine art
I have from their confines call'd to enact
My present fancies.

speeches.speech: Let me live here ever; So rare a wonder'd father and a wife Makes this place Paradise. [Juno and Ceres whisper, and send Iris on] employment]
lines: Let me live here ever;
So rare a wonder'd father and a wife
Makes this place Paradise.
[Juno and Ceres whisper, and send Iris on]
employment]

speeches.speech: Sweet, now, silence! Juno and Ceres whisper seriously; There's something else to do: hush, and be mute, Or else our spell is marr'd.
lines: Sweet, now, silence!
Juno and Ceres whisper seriously;
There's something else to do: hush, and be mute,
Or else our spell is marr'd.

speeches.speech: You nymphs, call'd Naiads, of the windring brooks, With your sedged crowns and ever-harmless looks, Leave your crisp channels and on this green land Answer your summons; Juno does command: Come, temperate nymphs, and help to celebrate A contract of true love; be not too late. [Enter certain Nymphs] You sunburnt sicklemen, of August weary, Come hither from the furrow and be merry: Make holiday; your rye-straw hats put on And these fresh nymphs encounter every one In country footing. [Enter certain Reapers, properly habited: they] join with the Nymphs in a graceful dance; towards the end whereof PROSPERO starts suddenly, and speaks; after which, to a strange, hollow, and confused noise, they heavily vanish]
lines: You nymphs, call'd Naiads, of the windring brooks,
With your sedged crowns and ever-harmless looks,
Leave your crisp channels and on this green land
Answer your summons; Juno does command:
Come, temperate nymphs, and help to celebrate
A contract of true love; be not too late.
[Enter certain Nymphs]
You sunburnt sicklemen, of August weary,
Come hither from the furrow and be merry:
Make holiday; your rye-straw hats put on
And these fresh nymphs encounter every one
In country footing.
[Enter certain Reapers, properly habited: they]
join with the Nymphs in a graceful dance;
towards the end whereof PROSPERO starts
suddenly, and speaks; after which, to a
strange, hollow, and confused noise, they
heavily vanish]

speeches.speech: [Aside] I had forgot that foul conspiracy Of the beast Caliban and his confederates Against my life: the minute of their plot Is almost come. [To the Spirits] Well done! avoid; no more!
lines: [Aside] I had forgot that foul conspiracy
Of the beast Caliban and his confederates
Against my life: the minute of their plot
Is almost come.
[To the Spirits]
Well done! avoid; no more!

speeches.speech: This is strange: your father's in some passion That works him strongly.
lines: This is strange: your father's in some passion
That works him strongly.

speeches.speech: Never till this day Saw I him touch'd with anger so distemper'd.
lines: Never till this day
Saw I him touch'd with anger so distemper'd.

speeches.speech: You do look, my son, in a moved sort, As if you were dismay'd: be cheerful, sir. Our revels now are ended. These our actors, As I foretold you, were all spirits and Are melted into air, into thin air: And, like the baseless fabric of this vision, The cloud-capp'd towers, the gorgeous palaces, The solemn temples, the great globe itself, Ye all which it inherit, shall dissolve And, like this insubstantial pageant faded, Leave not a rack behind. We are such stuff As dreams are made on, and our little life Is rounded with a sleep. Sir, I am vex'd; Bear with my weakness; my, brain is troubled: Be not disturb'd with my infirmity: If you be pleased, retire into my cell And there repose: a turn or two I'll walk, To still my beating mind.
lines: You do look, my son, in a moved sort,
As if you were dismay'd: be cheerful, sir.
Our revels now are ended. These our actors,
As I foretold you, were all spirits and
Are melted into air, into thin air:
And, like the baseless fabric of this vision,
The cloud-capp'd towers, the gorgeous palaces,
The solemn temples, the great globe itself,
Ye all which it inherit, shall dissolve
And, like this insubstantial pageant faded,
Leave not a rack behind. We are such stuff
As dreams are made on, and our little life
Is rounded with a sleep. Sir, I am vex'd;
Bear with my weakness; my, brain is troubled:
Be not disturb'd with my infirmity:
If you be pleased, retire into my cell
And there repose: a turn or two I'll walk,
To still my beating mind.

speeches.speech: [with Miranda] We wish your peace.
lines: [with Miranda] We wish your peace.

speeches.speech: Come with a thought I thank thee, Ariel: come.
lines: Come with a thought I thank thee, Ariel: come.

speeches.speech: Thy thoughts I cleave to. What's thy pleasure?
lines: Thy thoughts I cleave to. What's thy pleasure?

speeches.speech: Spirit, We must prepare to meet with Caliban.
lines: Spirit,
We must prepare to meet with Caliban.

speeches.speech: Ay, my commander: when I presented Ceres, I thought to have told thee of it, but I fear'd Lest I might anger thee.
lines: Ay, my commander: when I presented Ceres,
I thought to have told thee of it, but I fear'd
Lest I might anger thee.

speeches.speech: Say again, where didst thou leave these varlets?
lines: Say again, where didst thou leave these varlets?

speeches.speech: I told you, sir, they were red-hot with drinking; So fun of valour that they smote the air For breathing in their faces; beat the ground For kissing of their feet; yet always bending Towards their project. Then I beat my tabour; At which, like unback'd colts, they prick'd their ears, Advanced their eyelids, lifted up their noses As they smelt music: so I charm'd their ears That calf-like they my lowing follow'd through Tooth'd briers, sharp furzes, pricking goss and thorns, Which entered their frail shins: at last I left them I' the filthy-mantled pool beyond your cell, There dancing up to the chins, that the foul lake O'erstunk their feet.
lines: I told you, sir, they were red-hot with drinking;
So fun of valour that they smote the air
For breathing in their faces; beat the ground
For kissing of their feet; yet always bending
Towards their project. Then I beat my tabour;
At which, like unback'd colts, they prick'd
their ears,
Advanced their eyelids, lifted up their noses
As they smelt music: so I charm'd their ears
That calf-like they my lowing follow'd through
Tooth'd briers, sharp furzes, pricking goss and thorns,
Which entered their frail shins: at last I left them
I' the filthy-mantled pool beyond your cell,
There dancing up to the chins, that the foul lake
O'erstunk their feet.

speeches.speech: This was well done, my bird. Thy shape invisible retain thou still: The trumpery in my house, go bring it hither, For stale to catch these thieves.
lines: This was well done, my bird.
Thy shape invisible retain thou still:
The trumpery in my house, go bring it hither,
For stale to catch these thieves.

speeches.speech: I go, I go.
lines: I go, I go.

speeches.speech: A devil, a born devil, on whose nature Nurture can never stick; on whom my pains, Humanely taken, all, all lost, quite lost; And as with age his body uglier grows, So his mind cankers. I will plague them all, Even to roaring. [Re-enter ARIEL, loaden with glistering apparel, &c] Come, hang them on this line. [PROSPERO and ARIEL remain invisible. Enter] CALIBAN, STEPHANO, and TRINCULO, all wet]
lines: A devil, a born devil, on whose nature
Nurture can never stick; on whom my pains,
Humanely taken, all, all lost, quite lost;
And as with age his body uglier grows,
So his mind cankers. I will plague them all,
Even to roaring.
[Re-enter ARIEL, loaden with glistering apparel, &c]
Come, hang them on this line.
[PROSPERO and ARIEL remain invisible. Enter]
CALIBAN, STEPHANO, and TRINCULO, all wet]

speeches.speech: Pray you, tread softly, that the blind mole may not Hear a foot fall: we now are near his cell.
lines: Pray you, tread softly, that the blind mole may not
Hear a foot fall: we now are near his cell.

speeches.speech: Monster, your fairy, which you say is a harmless fairy, has done little better than played the Jack with us.
lines: Monster, your fairy, which you say is
a harmless fairy, has done little better than
played the Jack with us.

speeches.speech: Monster, I do smell all horse-piss; at which my nose is in great indignation.
lines: Monster, I do smell all horse-piss; at
which my nose is in great indignation.

speeches.speech: So is mine. Do you hear, monster? If I should take a displeasure against you, look you,--
lines: So is mine. Do you hear, monster? If I should take
a displeasure against you, look you,--

speeches.speech: Thou wert but a lost monster.
lines: Thou wert but a lost monster.

speeches.speech: Good my lord, give me thy favour still. Be patient, for the prize I'll bring thee to Shall hoodwink this mischance: therefore speak softly. All's hush'd as midnight yet.
lines: Good my lord, give me thy favour still.
Be patient, for the prize I'll bring thee to
Shall hoodwink this mischance: therefore speak softly.
All's hush'd as midnight yet.

speeches.speech: Ay, but to lose our bottles in the pool,--
lines: Ay, but to lose our bottles in the pool,--

speeches.speech: There is not only disgrace and dishonour in that, monster, but an infinite loss.
lines: There is not only disgrace and dishonour in that,
monster, but an infinite loss.

speeches.speech: That's more to me than my wetting: yet this is your harmless fairy, monster.
lines: That's more to me than my wetting: yet this is your
harmless fairy, monster.

speeches.speech: I will fetch off my bottle, though I be o'er ears for my labour.
lines: I will fetch off my bottle, though I be o'er ears
for my labour.

speeches.speech: Prithee, my king, be quiet. Seest thou here, This is the mouth o' the cell: no noise, and enter. Do that good mischief which may make this island Thine own for ever, and I, thy Caliban, For aye thy foot-licker.
lines: Prithee, my king, be quiet. Seest thou here,
This is the mouth o' the cell: no noise, and enter.
Do that good mischief which may make this island
Thine own for ever, and I, thy Caliban,
For aye thy foot-licker.

speeches.speech: Give me thy hand. I do begin to have bloody thoughts.
lines: Give me thy hand. I do begin to have bloody thoughts.

speeches.speech: O king Stephano! O peer! O worthy Stephano! look what a wardrobe here is for thee!
lines: O king Stephano! O peer! O worthy Stephano! look
what a wardrobe here is for thee!

speeches.speech: Let it alone, thou fool; it is but trash.
lines: Let it alone, thou fool; it is but trash.

speeches.speech: O, ho, monster! we know what belongs to a frippery. O king Stephano!
lines: O, ho, monster! we know what belongs to a frippery.
O king Stephano!

speeches.speech: Put off that gown, Trinculo; by this hand, I'll have that gown.
lines: Put off that gown, Trinculo; by this hand, I'll have
that gown.

speeches.speech: Thy grace shall have it.
lines: Thy grace shall have it.

speeches.speech: The dropsy drown this fool I what do you mean To dote thus on such luggage? Let's alone And do the murder first: if he awake, From toe to crown he'll fill our skins with pinches, Make us strange stuff.
lines: The dropsy drown this fool I what do you mean
To dote thus on such luggage? Let's alone
And do the murder first: if he awake,
From toe to crown he'll fill our skins with pinches,
Make us strange stuff.

speeches.speech: Be you quiet, monster. Mistress line, is not this my jerkin? Now is the jerkin under the line: now, jerkin, you are like to lose your hair and prove a bald jerkin.
lines: Be you quiet, monster. Mistress line,
is not this my jerkin? Now is the jerkin under
the line: now, jerkin, you are like to lose your
hair and prove a bald jerkin.

speeches.speech: Do, do: we steal by line and level, an't like your grace.
lines: Do, do: we steal by line and level, an't like your grace.

speeches.speech: I thank thee for that jest; here's a garment for't: wit shall not go unrewarded while I am king of this country. 'Steal by line and level' is an excellent pass of pate; there's another garment for't.
lines: I thank thee for that jest; here's a garment for't:
wit shall not go unrewarded while I am king of this
country. 'Steal by line and level' is an excellent
pass of pate; there's another garment for't.

speeches.speech: Monster, come, put some lime upon your fingers, and away with the rest.
lines: Monster, come, put some lime upon your fingers, and
away with the rest.

speeches.speech: I will have none on't: we shall lose our time, And all be turn'd to barnacles, or to apes With foreheads villanous low.
lines: I will have none on't: we shall lose our time,
And all be turn'd to barnacles, or to apes
With foreheads villanous low.

speeches.speech: Monster, lay-to your fingers: help to bear this away where my hogshead of wine is, or I'll turn you out of my kingdom: go to, carry this.
lines: Monster, lay-to your fingers: help to bear this
away where my hogshead of wine is, or I'll turn you
out of my kingdom: go to, carry this.

speeches.speech: And this.
lines: And this.

speeches.speech: Ay, and this. [A noise of hunters heard. Enter divers Spirits,] in shape of dogs and hounds, and hunt them about, PROSPERO and ARIEL setting them on]
lines: Ay, and this.
[A noise of hunters heard. Enter divers Spirits,]
in shape of dogs and hounds, and hunt them about,
PROSPERO and ARIEL setting them on]

speeches.speech: Hey, Mountain, hey!
lines: Hey, Mountain, hey!

speeches.speech: Silver I there it goes, Silver!
lines: Silver I there it goes, Silver!

speeches.speech: Fury, Fury! there, Tyrant, there! hark! hark! [CALIBAN, STEPHANO, and TRINCULO, are] driven out] Go charge my goblins that they grind their joints With dry convulsions, shorten up their sinews With aged cramps, and more pinch-spotted make them Than pard or cat o' mountain.
lines: Fury, Fury! there, Tyrant, there! hark! hark!
[CALIBAN, STEPHANO, and TRINCULO, are]
driven out]
Go charge my goblins that they grind their joints
With dry convulsions, shorten up their sinews
With aged cramps, and more pinch-spotted make them
Than pard or cat o' mountain.

speeches.speech: Hark, they roar!
lines: Hark, they roar!

speeches.speech: Let them be hunted soundly. At this hour Lie at my mercy all mine enemies: Shortly shall all my labours end, and thou Shalt have the air at freedom: for a little Follow, and do me service.
